data.title: Annual trading volume increased 80-fold: How Kalshi reshaped the sports prediction market
data.description:
Author: Kunal Doshi, Blockworks Research; Translator: Felix, PANews

This report analyzes Kalshi's rapid growth and the evolving structure of the sports prediction market, comparing its pricing, liquidity, and valuation against traditional sports betting companies. Furthermore, this article assesses whether these markets offer viable trading opportunities and what is driving their adoption.

Prediction markets have rapidly evolved from niche products into a significant underlying financial instrument. While their early large-scale adoption was driven by the 2024 US election, these markets have since expanded into new categories, with sports events emerging as one of the largest and fastest-growing segments. This shift occurs against the backdrop of a US sports betting market that continues to grow at $167 billion, but whose structure remains unfavorable to participants due to built-in rake and bookmaker-controlled pricing.

In contrast, prediction markets operate on a peer-to-peer trading model, where prices reflect probabilities set by participants rather than odds set by market makers. The pricing mechanism is more transparent, similar to financial markets. In financial markets, price discovery is driven by information flow and participant behavior. Kalshi's Growth and Market Structure Kalshi experienced rapid growth over the past year, with monthly nominal trading volume increasing 80-fold from $180 million at the beginning of 2025 to $14.4 billion in March 2026. The average monthly trading volume growth rate reached 40%, reflecting increased user engagement and trading activity. The sports market has been a major driver of this growth, currently accounting for 68% of total trading volume. Trading volume in the sports sector is projected to grow 18-fold in three quarters, from $540 million in July 2025 to $9.9 billion in March 2026. Kalshi holds a strong position in this sector, accounting for approximately 70% of the sports prediction market's trading volume. Meanwhile, the composition of trading volume is also becoming more diversified. As other categories such as cryptocurrencies, entertainment, and elections gain traction, the share of sports events has begun to decline from its peak of 90% in September 2025. This diversification reduces reliance on a single category and contributes to more stable long-term growth. Within sports trading, volume is significantly influenced by seasonality and driven by major events. Basketball accounts for 44% of sports trading volume, followed by rugby (28%) and tennis (10%). American football drove a dramatic increase in betting volume between August and September 2025, surging from $640 million to $2.6 billion, before declining after the Super Bowl. Basketball has consistently topped the betting charts since October, with the recent NCAA Men's Basketball Championship generating $1.5 billion in actual betting volume and $3.3 billion in nominal betting volume. This is comparable to the estimated $3.3 billion in betting by Americans during "March Madness" in 2026, highlighting the increasingly important role of prediction markets. Pricing Accuracy and Information Efficiency Prediction markets aim to reflect true probabilities. To assess this, this paper compares the implied probabilities two days before the game with the actual results. In an efficient market, a team with 70% odds should have approximately a 70% chance of winning. The focus is on the basketball and NFL (Major National Football League) markets, which together account for 72% of Kalshi Sports' trading volume. This analysis is based on 3,100 games and involves $8.5 billion in nominal trading volume. Two days before a game, Kalshi prices are a strong indicator of the game's outcome. On average, the implied probability deviates from the actual result by 5.5 percentage points. Among the four leagues analyzed, NCAA college football had the highest prediction accuracy. This indicates that pre-game odds are a strong indicator of the final result. Kalshi prices are not only reactive but also contain important pre-game information. This accuracy may reflect the presence of informed individuals and model-driven traders who actively price probabilities based on available data. Therefore, market prices begin to converge towards the actual result well before the game starts, reinforcing the role of prediction markets as an effective information aggregation mechanism. Liquidity, Volume Distribution, and Market Maker Behavior Analyzing the distribution of trading volume throughout the sports market cycle is crucial. Volume distribution helps reveal when valuable information enters the market, how price discovery evolves, and who is driving market volatility. For traders, this highlights when mispricing is likely and when pricing is effective. For market makers, it provides a basis for setting spreads, managing inventory risk, and choosing the timing for aggressive or passive pricing. As expected, approximately 85% of trading volume occurs during the game itself, as real-time events continuously update the probabilities of each team's victory. About 3% of the trading volume occurs in the final 30 minutes, while trading volume is almost zero in the final 10 minutes, as the game's outcome is largely determined at this point. The absence of a final surge in trading volume suggests that most participants hold positions until the end of the game rather than actively closing them. Pre-match trading activity is concentrated in two time periods. Trading volume in the 1 to 48 hours before the match accounts for 10.4% of total volume, while trading volume in the last hour before the match accounts for 4%. Before this, trading activity is minimal, accounting for only 1.3%. This indicates that approximately 48 hours before the match, the market transitions from a low-liquidity, low-information environment to a more active price discovery phase, at which point informed or model-driven participants begin to build positions. The surge in trading volume at the last minute may reflect the consolidation of last-minute information such as lineups and news. Based on this distribution, we can assess how market makers adjust their pricing behavior. Market depth data became available on March 25th, so the analysis focuses on the "March Madness" competition, during which trading volume surged. Spreads initially widened but narrowed to around 1.11% in the approximately 48 hours before the competition began, and remained near the lower limit of 1% until the competition ended. This aligns with the increase in trading volume, indicating that market makers were more active and competitive during this period. Within the 48-hour window before the competition, market depth and trading volume increased in tandem by 19 times. However, during the competition, despite more frequent trading activity, depth decreased by 76%. This indicates that market makers reduced order sizes while maintaining tight spreads, likely to manage adverse selection risk during rapid probability updates in live competition. During the competition, the average contract depth per market was 163,000 contracts, compared to a pre-competition peak of 730,000 contracts. At a typical price close to $0.50, this translates to approximately $4,100 of executable liquidity per side when a trade is triggered. This is sufficient for small retail trades but becomes a constraint for larger institutional orders. For institutional participants with $5,000 or more invested per position, execution capacity during the match is limited because orders either impact prices or must be spread across multiple markets. Therefore, for larger traders, the optimal execution window is 48 hours to 1 hour before the match, when contract depth is more than four times that during the match and spreads remain relatively small. Comparison with Traditional Sports Betting and Arbitrage Dynamics Predicting market prices must accurately reflect live match conditions to attract sophisticated traders and institutional funds. To assess this, this article compares the live odds of Kalshi and FanDuel (traditional betting platforms) for two high-volume matches. University of Michigan vs. University of Arizona, NCAA Tournament. In the 72 hours leading up to the game, Kalshi and FanDuel's predictions were very close, with Arizona's implied winning percentage around 48% on both platforms. About 18 hours before the game, FanDuel adjusted Arizona's winning percentage to nearly 50%, and Kalshi made the same adjustment about 7 hours later. The average pre-game difference was 0.54%, indicating a high degree of consistency in pre-game predictions. After the game started, prices moved almost synchronously, with a correlation of 0.9962. This indicates that there was no significant lead or lag between the two platforms within 5 minutes. However, a persistent pricing gap emerged, with a median difference of 2.25% during the trade. This difference primarily stems from the different platform structures. FanDuel incorporates approximately 4.5% margin (or rake), equivalent to about 2.25% per side. On the Kalshi platform, contracts are traded in 1% increments, with a minimum spread of 1%. For the taker in an arbitrage trade, the actual cost of traversing the spread is approximately 0.5%, resulting in a total trading friction of approximately 2.75%. Therefore, traders must overcome these combined costs for arbitrage opportunities to be profitable.

In reality, the price difference only slightly offset these costs. The median price difference during the game was 2.59%, exceeding the cost threshold in 8 out of 20 five-minute intervals (40%). While the price difference reached 3.64% at one point in the second half due to FanDuel's slower repricing of Michigan's widening lead, these opportunities were not long enough to support a reliable arbitrage strategy. Furthermore, Kalshi's lack of in-game liquidity further limited the realization of effective scalable arbitrage.

It is noteworthy that the price difference in the second half was directional, with FanDuel consistently pricing Arizona higher than Kalshi as Michigan widened its lead.

This indicates that Kalshi was able to integrate in-game information more quickly during the peak of the game. In the Buffalo Bills vs. Denver Broncos NFL playoffs game, pre-game pricing did not immediately converge. Five days before the game, the two platforms' predictions differed by 5.7%, with FanDuel predicting a 53.7% win rate for the Buffalo Bills and Kalshi predicting 48%. Over the following week, both platforms gradually adjusted their predictions, with neither consistently leading. By the start of the game, both platforms predicted a close 48% win rate for the Buffalo Bills. The average pre-game difference was 1.40%, more than double that of similar cases in the NCAAB (National Collegiate Football Association), indicating weak early consensus. After the match began, prices were closely correlated again, with a correlation of 0.9738. This match was exceptionally intense, with the lead changing hands multiple times and win rates fluctuating significantly. For example, the Buffalo team's win rate reached 74% late in overtime, but the Denver Broncos ultimately won. The median difference during the match was 1.61%, and the average was 2.40%. Unlike the NCAAB example, the direction of the difference in this match was not clearly predictable, with the two platforms alternating between leading and trailing. At a cost threshold of 2.75%, 13 out of 45 five-minute timeframes (29%) presented sufficient spreads for trading. These opportunities were concentrated during periods of high price volatility, with spreads reaching as high as 8.8%, but lasting only a short time. Similar to the NCAAB example, insufficient intra-match liquidity on the Kalshi platform limited the effective trade size and reduced the practical feasibility of arbitrage. What does this mean for market participants? Kalshi's odds are closely correlated with traditional sports betting companies, and both platforms integrate match information in near real-time. The odds were highly consistent before and during the match, although some minor and persistent discrepancies remained. This highlights Kalshi's strength in information aggregation and generating accurate probabilities. FanDuel's odds deducted commissions, which amount to approximately 2% of the entry cost per side. On the Kalshi platform, fees vary depending on the contract probability, peaking at around 50% probability levels. For takers, the fee is approximately 3.50%, while for place orders, it's approximately 0.88%. Since most trades occur within a 40% to 60% probability range, the actual transaction cost on the Kalshi platform is between 2.8% and 4.2%, comparable to, or even higher than, traditional sports betting platforms, especially for takers. These higher costs, coupled with thinner in-game liquidity, mean that larger, more sophisticated betting players are likely to continue to prefer traditional bookmakers, where pricing is often negotiable and large trades are executed more efficiently. To improve its competitiveness, Kalshi needs to lower fees and strengthen liquidity incentives, especially during in-game trading, when trading activity is high but depth is low. However, these limitations may improve over time. Increasing competition from platforms like Polymarket should drive changes in fee structures and liquidity incentives. In the sports betting market, Polymarket charges a taker fee of 0.03, lower than Kalshi's 0.07. Polymarket also offers zero fees and a 25% fee rebate to order placers. Furthermore, Polymarket announced a $5 million sports liquidity incentive program, launching in April 2026, designed to encourage tighter pricing and deeper order books. While Kalshi also offers a liquidity incentive program, its base fee structure remains relatively high. Increased competition among platforms should drive fee reductions and improve overall market liquidity. This, in turn, will create a virtuous cycle. Improved liquidity attracts more traders, increased trading volume attracts more market makers, and increased market-making activity further deepens liquidity and improves execution quality. As market depth improves and execution becomes more efficient over time, more sophisticated capital is expected to participate, including hedge funds focused on sports events and reinsurance-like players. Early signs of this trend are emerging, with Kalshi expanding its business into the reinsurance market through partnerships with institutions such as Game Point Capital. Beyond trading, the probability data on the Kalshi platform is also beginning to demonstrate its informational value. In the sports market, clubs, agents, or reinsurance companies can leverage market-implied probabilities to hedge contract risks, such as performance bonuses linked to player or team results. Kalshi's Structural Advantages Despite its pricing mechanism being similar to traditional sports betting companies and sometimes having higher transaction costs, Kalshi has achieved rapid expansion thanks to several structural advantages over traditional sports betting companies. First, prediction markets employ a peer-to-peer trading model, where traders trade against each other rather than betting against a house with an inherent advantage. This structure allows Kalshi to be regulated by the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) as a designated contract market, enabling it to operate in all 50 states. In contrast, major U.S. sports betting companies like DraftKings and FanDuel only offer services in select states, while platforms like Pinnacle and Betfair remain unavailable in the U.S., giving Kalshi a much broader potential market. Second, traditional sports betting companies often limit betting amounts to discourage high-performing bettors from investing large sums. These limits are typically dynamic, with high-performing bettors potentially facing reduced betting amounts or account restrictions. This creates a structural ceiling on returns. Kalshi, without such restrictions, benefits from the participation of more sophisticated traders who contribute to improved price accuracy and overall market efficiency. Secondly, Kalshi's interface presents results directly as probabilities rather than odds, making pricing more intuitive and easier to understand. This aligns better with how institutional participants assess expected value and risk. Furthermore, Kalshi supports continuous trading, allowing users to dynamically open and close positions throughout the entire match. While sports betting websites may offer early cash-out features, these are not universally available and are often limited at crucial moments. Despite this, Kalshi's liquidity is not consistently stable throughout the match. According to discussions with market makers, liquidity may decrease at critical moments (such as shots or key actions), and some liquidity providers may temporarily withdraw their offers while awaiting settlement. However, users can still close positions during these periods, although some slippage may occur during execution. Valuation Assessment Given the above, the key question is how Kalshi's valuation compares to traditional sports betting operators. Using Kalshi's fee calculation formula: 0.07 × number of shares × stock price × (1 − stock price), we can estimate its revenue generated in various markets. This formula does not include certain fee-free markets, differences in market maker fees, etc. Furthermore, this formula does not include trading volume through partners such as Robinhood, as these trading volumes require revenue sharing. Based on this, Kalshi's revenue last year was approximately $263 million, and it is projected to reach $1.3 billion by 2026, representing a monthly growth rate of 24%. Approximately 80% to 90% of its revenue comes from the sports betting market. Based on its $22 billion valuation in its last funding round, this implies a projected price-to-sales ratio of approximately 16.9 times in 2026. In contrast, traditional sports betting operators like DraftKings (DKNG) and Flutter Entertainment (FLUT) have significantly higher absolute revenues, projected to reach $6.1 billion and $16.4 billion respectively by 2025. Despite this, their market capitalizations are $11 billion and $17.9 billion respectively, meaning their valuation multiples are significantly lower. Historically, these companies have typically had price-to-sales ratios between 2 and 4. This valuation gap reflects a difference in how the market positions Kalshi. While their basic uses are similar—both based on analyzing event results—Kalshi's valuation is closer to that of an exchange than a sports betting company. This is primarily due to its peer-to-peer trading structure, its regulation by the US CFTC, and its ability to expand into areas such as cryptocurrency, macroeconomics, and political markets. These factors imply a larger potential market and higher long-term growth potential. Kalshi's growth prospects further support its premium. While DraftKings and Flutter have seen continued revenue growth, their growth rates have slowed significantly in recent years. In contrast, Kalshi's growth rate is remarkable, with monthly growth rates exceeding the annual growth rates of these traditional operators. Valuation differences across the industry also reflect broader market dynamics. Betting companies tend to have lower valuation multiples due to regulatory risks, taxation, and profit margin pressures. In contrast, exchanges like Coinbase and Robinhood have higher valuation multiples, suggesting that investors value Kalshi similarly. This market positioning aligns with its market structure, where participants trade against each other rather than betting against a house. However, some risks exist. Traditional sports betting companies are actively exploring entry into the prediction market, which could erode Kalshi's early lead. The recent regulatory hurdles these operators have faced may reflect their attempt to delay the arrival of competition while building their own products. Furthermore, it is estimated that a significant portion of Kalshi's trading volume comes from distribution partners such as Robinhood. Control over end-users is a key driver of long-term value, and any move by a partner to launch a competing product could impact Kalshi's growth trajectory. In summary, Kalshi has become a leading sports prediction market platform, with prices closely tracking real-time dynamics and gradually approaching true probabilities before events begin. This pricing accuracy and information efficiency make it a reliable alternative to traditional sports betting companies. While currently high fees and limited on-exchange liquidity restrict trade execution, especially for large trades, these issues are expected to improve as competition drives down fees and liquidity incentives increase. Increased market depth will create a virtuous cycle of higher trading volumes, smaller spreads, and more active market-making, further enhancing market quality. Sports events are a major driver of trading volume and user engagement, and Kalshi's exchange-like structure and ability to attract participants provide a solid foundation for its continued growth. Its valuation reflects this market position. However, maintaining this high valuation will depend on its ability to effectively scale liquidity and retain its role as a core hub in the sports events market.

data.title: Kelp suffered a nearly $300 million theft; Aave took on $200 million in bad debt.
data.description:
Author: Sanqing, Foresight News

In the early hours of April 19th, the Kelp DAO product, based on LayerZero, compromised its rsETH cross-chain bridge. The attacker called the lzReceive method of the LayerZero EndpointV2 contract, forging a cross-chain message that caused the mainnet OFTAdapter to release 116,500 rsETH to the attacker's address. At the time, this was worth approximately $292 million, representing 18% of the approximately 630,000 circulating rsETH. The attacker's wallet had deposited funds through the Tornado Cash 1 ETH pool 10 hours before the attack.

Within one hour, Kelp urgently executed pauseAll, freezing the LRT Deposit Pool, the Withdrawal contract, the LRT Oracle, and the rsETH tokens themselves.

The attackers subsequently launched two follow-up attacks, each attempting to steal another 40,000 rsETH, but both were rolled back due to the contract being suspended; otherwise, the total loss would have approached $391 million. This is the largest single DeFi loss so far in 2026, surpassing the $285 million Drift Protocol hack on April 1st. According to Bitget market data, after the incident, AAVE, ZRO, and KERNEL fell by approximately 16%, 20%, and 11% respectively in the past 24 hours. The locked amount in OFTAdapter represents all of it. Kelp's cross-chain solution is a typical Hub-and-Spoke deployment. The mainnet retains the minting and redemption rights of rsETH through the OFTAdapter contract, and over 20 L2 transactions are mapped using standard OFT contracts. Cross-chain transactions do not generate wrapped versions; instead, they involve a 1:1 debit-credit settlement. From the mainnet to L2, the process involves Adapter locking and L2 minting; from L2 to the mainnet, it involves L2 burning and Adapter release. The entire ledger is maintained by the LayerZero message layer, and the Kelp contract is only responsible for executing the lock/burn/mint/unlock actions based on the message. `lzReceive` is the crucial component of this mechanism. It is the entry point for EndpointV2 to call the target chain's OFT/OFTAdapter, and theoretically, it only accepts messages verified by LayerZero. The attacker bypassed the verification logic, forging a cross-chain message without a corresponding source chain burn record, directly triggering the mainnet Adapter's release, causing 116,500 rsETH to flow out of reserves without any burn offset.

Without a source debit, a target-side credit occurred. This is the moment when the omnichain supply conservation was broken.

In this structure, the locked staking in the mainnet Adapter contract is the ultimate value support for all rsETH on the 20+ chains. rsETH on L2 is never an independent asset; it's merely a withdrawal order to the mainnet reserves. After the reserves are depleted, all withdrawal orders become worthless.

Furthermore, the attack's success may also be related to Kelp's choice of a 1/1 DVN configuration, where cross-chain messages only require a single validator signature to pass, representing the weakest security level allowed by LayerZero.

As early as January 2025, a development team reminded Kelp on the Aave governance forum that it should expand to multiple DVN verifications. Fifteen months later, the second DVN has still not been added. Bridge reserves are a more vulnerable single point of failure than single-chain contracts. However, under pressure to expand market share, LRT generally opted for the rapid multi-chain deployment of OFT-style tokens, rather than the slower but more secure native minting path. Speed ​​brought market share, but also this financial burden. Aave's bad debts are the real bleeding point. The $292 million is not just a problem for Kelp alone; the real structural risks unfolded in the latter half of the attack. The attackers deposited the stolen rsETH into DeFi lending platforms such as Aave V3, V4, Compound V3, and Euler, using it as collateral to lend WETH/ETH. On Aave alone, approximately $196 million was lent out, bringing the total debt position to over $236 million. On-chain tracking data shows that the attackers consolidated approximately 74,000 ETH into a single address, of which approximately $250 million has been converted into ETH. The collateral for these lending positions, rsETH, was emptied from the mainnet Adapter reserves the moment it was deposited into Aave. Lacking real value, the collateral could not be disposed of through normal liquidation mechanisms, and Aave's WETH lending pool directly bore the bad debt. The Aave team initially stated that they would use the Umbrella security reserve to offset any bad debts, but later revised their wording on X to "explore paths to offset the deficit," a subtle step back. The cost of whitelisting LRT for lending protocols is being priced into their balance sheets today. The LRT collateral whitelist should be rewritten. Besides Aave, SparkLend and Fluid simultaneously froze the rsETH market. Upshift suspended deposits and withdrawals in the High Growth ETH and Kelp Gain vaults. Lido Earn suspended further deposits due to earnETH's exposure to rsETH, while emphasizing that the stETH and wstETH core protocols are unaffected. Ethena has no exposure to rsETH and, out of caution, has suspended its LayerZero OFT bridge for approximately 6 hours. At least 9 protocols have triggered emergency responses. The spread of the contagion chain to such a dense number of nodes within hours is not due to a risk control failure of a single protocol, but rather a direct result of the over-combination of LRT as collateral. The longer the yield stacking, with each layer of staking, re-staking, cross-chain deployment, and lending collateral, it means that another trust assumption is implicitly accepted. When the reserves of the bottom-level Adapter are emptied, the entire chain becomes unbalanced. Previously, Kelp experienced an over-minting event in April 2025 due to a bug in the fee contract, at which time user funds were not lost. A year later, it proved in a more costly way that the risks of LRT never stemmed solely from the smart contracts themselves, but from the "efficiency assumptions" repeatedly staked, crossed chains, and combined throughout the DeFi stack. In the first four months of 2026, DeFi attack losses approached $1 billion, with Drift and Kelp contributing two incidents exceeding $280 million each. Following this event, lending protocols should treat LRT-like assets with caution, at least reducing their collateral parameters.

data.title: Aave incurred $200 million in bad debts shortly after its core risk control team was dismissed.
data.description:
Author: 0x2333

In the early morning of April 18, 2026, a few hours after the KelpDAO attack, Solidity developer 0xQuit posted a message on X.

... "Hopefully I can bring better news, but WETH on Aave seems to be finished. It's possible to withdraw it if possible, but it might be too late. Normal deposits should be partially withdrawable after the Umbrella settlement. This is a huge blow to the DeFi vision." This post was made just as Aave founder Stani Kulechov issued another statement on the same platform: rsETH has been frozen, Aave's smart contracts are "undamaged," and the problem lies with the Kelp DAO. The two posts scrolled side-by-side on the same timeline. Both posts state the facts. However, they answer different questions. Stani answers who tampered with the code, while 0xQuit answers who bears the consequences. The answer is: no code was touched. The consequences fell on everyone who deposited WETH into Aave, thinking they were just earning a little interest. In the six months leading up to the attack, Aave's governance system approved every decision that made this possible. No one cracked any code. Someone used an approved set of rules to cause the protocol to collapse as designed. This needs to be explained from the beginning.

Twelve Days

On April 6th, Chaos Labs founder Omer Goldberg announced on X that the collaboration between Chaos Labs and Aave DAO would officially end.

For the past three years, Chaos Labs has led the management of Aave's risk parameters. During this period, Aave's TVL grew from $5.2 billion to over $26 billion. Behind every $100 million increase, Chaos Labs' model was calculating the boundaries: which parameters could be pushed forward, and which could not.

Goldberg gave three reasons for leaving. First, a "fundamental disagreement" on risk strategy, especially after Aave V4 introduced a new architecture. Second, the operational complexity brought by V4 increased significantly, but resource compensation did not keep up.

Third, even with a $5 million budget, Chaos Labs remains unprofitable and economically unsustainable. "This partnership no longer reflects how we believe risk should be managed," he wrote. Aave's response was swift. Stani Kulechov stated that the agreement would not disrupt operations, and that risk management firm LlamaRisk would assume full responsibility, "maintaining the two-tiered risk management system." LlamaRisk subsequently issued a statement promising "full operational continuity" and submitted a formal renewal proposal to the Aave DAO within a week. From the outside, this appeared to be an orderly transition. Three days later, on April 9th, LlamaRisk, as the new risk manager, submitted its first routine adjustments: increasing the supply cap of rsETH on the Aave V3 mainnet from 480,000 to 530,000. The basis for this was on-chain data: healthy utilization, sufficient liquidity, and position concentration within the threshold. There were no anomaly markers. Nine days later, on April 18th at 17:35 UTC, the attacker invoked LayerZero's EndpointV2 contract on the Ethereum mainnet, sending a forged cross-chain data packet to the Kelp DAO's rsETH bridge contract. The bridge contract failed to recognize the message as fake. 116,500 rsETH flowed to an address controlled by the attacker. Forty-six minutes later, Kelp DAO's emergency suspension mechanism activated, preventing the attacker from conducting two additional theft attempts, which together amounted to approximately $100 million. However, the initial batch is irretrievable. The attackers targeted approximately $390 million, and they obtained three-quarters of it. Before the suspension mechanism was activated, the attackers had deposited the stolen rsETH into Aave V3 as collateral and borrowed a large amount of WETH and ETH. The market price of rsETH began to collapse after news of the attack spread, and the value of the collateral evaporated. Positions that were technically valid became unmanageable. Bad debts were thus created. The Unwritten Document On January 19, 2026, the Aave community passed governance proposal 434. The core of the proposal was to add WETH to rsETH's LST E-Mode, while increasing the maximum loan-to-value ratio of rsETH in this mode from 92.5% to 93%. The numbers haven't changed much, but the meaning is clear: users can borrow $93 worth of WETH on Aave using $100 of rsETH. This proposal is driven by ACI (Aave Chan Initiative, Aave's core governance service). The proposal text states the expectation: by introducing an rsETH/WETH circulation strategy to absorb idle ETH liquidity in the protocol, it is expected to bring "up to $1 billion in rsETH inflows," while simultaneously bringing the utilization rate of the WETH pool back to its optimal range. The proposal also has another, more directly stated reason: to "maintain horizontal competition" with ezETH and weETH. Since competitors' LRT assets on Aave have already achieved similar parameters, rsETH should also align. This is a very common decision-making logic in DeFi called competitive benchmarking. What your competitors have, you should have too, otherwise liquidity will flow away. In a context that prioritizes capital efficiency, this logic is almost flawless. However, it also carries an inherent one-way pressure: parameters can only be increased, not decreased. Any proposal to tighten parameters is labeled as "weakening competitiveness." The result is that the entire industry drifts in the same direction without anyone questioning where it's drifting to. Looking at the governance documents of Proposal 434, one thing is missing: a risk assessment report specifically addressing the question, "Can rsETH's LTV be increased to 93%?" When rsETH first listed in November 2024, LlamaRisk submitted a comprehensive collateral risk assessment, analyzing rsETH's yield accumulation mechanism, smart contract structure, and liquidity characteristics. But that report answered the question, "Can rsETH be listed on Aave?" When Proposal 434 pushed the LTV to 93%, the governance documents relied on horizontal benchmarking and protocol revenue expectations. The other two DeFi protocols that accepted rsETH gave different answers. SparkLend set an LTV of 72% for rsETH, while the Fluid protocol used a fixed minimum collateralization rate equivalent to approximately 75% LTV. Both of these protocols completed the freezing of the rsETH market within hours after the attack. Aave's figure was 93%. The difference of 21 percentage points resulted in a competitive advantage. On April 6, Chaos Labs announced its withdrawal from Aave Risk Management. On April 9th, LlamaRisk, the newly acquired firm, submitted a routine Risk Stewards adjustment proposal, increasing the supply cap of rsETH from 480,000 to 530,000. The rationale was healthy on-chain data, normal utilization, sufficient liquidity, and acceptable position concentration. All metrics are on-chain. These on-chain metrics record the flow of rsETH within Aave: how many people are using it, whether the risk is diversified, and whether liquidity is sufficient. What they don't cover is: what kind of bridge rsETH crosses before reaching Aave. An Unread Alarm In the early morning of March 10th this year, a batch of unusual liquidation transactions began appearing on the Ethereum chain. 34 highly leveraged positions using wstETH as collateral triggered liquidation lines one after another without any warning. Before users could react, the liquidation bot had already completed the operation. The trigger was a configuration error in Aave's CAPO oracle system, causing a discrepancy between the snapshot ratio and the snapshot timestamp. This resulted in a reported price of approximately 1.1939 for wstETH, while the actual market exchange rate was approximately 1.228. The deviation was 2.85%, which is negligible under normal circumstances. However, in E-Mode, a 2.85% price undervaluation was enough to push 34 highly leveraged positions past the liquidation threshold, resulting in approximately $27 million in erroneous liquidation losses. From the suggestion issued by Chaos Labs' Edge Risk system to BGD's AgentHub executing it in the next block, and then to the liquidation bot completing the operation, the entire chain was completed within minutes. There was no window for human intervention. Afterwards, Chaos Labs released an analysis report. The conclusion was: "This incident does not reflect a flaw in the design of the underlying CAPO or off-chain risk oracle, but rather an inconsistency in snapshot ratios and timestamps caused by different update constraints in the on-chain configuration." A configuration issue, not a design problem. An accident, not a warning. Aave, through a governance proposal, fully compensated affected users from recovered funds and the DAO treasury. The matter was closed in this way. A later industry report stated, "Despite this incident, Aave's overall deposits and borrowings remained stable in early 2026, and confidence in the protocol's core design was not substantially weakened." Six weeks later, the "core design" claim will face another test, on a completely different scale. The bill arrived. About an hour after the attack, Stani Kulechov emphasized on X that Aave's smart contract itself was "undamaged." Technically, there was nothing wrong with this; no code was cracked, no private keys were stolen, and the contract functioned precisely as it was set up. The problem lies here. When rsETH de-pegged due to the attack and its value plummeted, E-Mode's "highly correlated" design backfired: the system continued to treat the already significantly devalued rsETH as valid collateral, and the lent WETH and ETH could not be properly liquidated. A mechanism designed to improve capital efficiency became a lock for bad debts in this extreme situation. The estimated size of the bad debt is between $177 million and $200 million (according to multiple sources including Phemex and Yahoo Finance), and the total amount of lending positions opened by the attackers exceeds $236 million (according to CryptoBriefing). With 116,500 rsETH as collateral, a maximum of approximately $272 million in WETH could be borrowed under 93% E-Mode LTV, about $62 million more than the standard 72% LTV limit. E-Mode compresses the safety buffer from 28% to 7%, meaning any slight price fluctuation could cause the positions to spiral out of control. Aave has a security mechanism specifically designed for this situation called Umbrella. Users can stake aWETH in Umbrella's secure vault to earn additional rewards. When the protocol experiences bad debt deficits, these assets are automatically destroyed to cover the losses, without requiring governance intervention. Users who actively choose to stake are mostly those who understand the mechanism's design, are willing to exchange their principal for higher returns, and are willing to assume the protocol's liability protection role; they are active supporters of the protocol. Umbrella launched at the end of 2025, replacing the old Safety Module, and this is its first real-world test. Approximately $50 million worth of WETH is available in Umbrella to absorb losses (according to Forbes). The bad debt is estimated at $177 million to $200 million. The gap between these two figures is approximately $127 million to $150 million. This portion is borne by ordinary WETH depositors who are not currently staking their assets. Aave's official documentation describes the Umbrella mechanism as follows: after the staking assets are destroyed, "remaining WETH suppliers should be able to withdraw some, but full recovery is not guaranteed, and depositors may face a haircut." A "haircut" means losing a portion of the principal. On the night of the attack, Marc Zeller spoke out. He is the founder of ACI and a key driver of proposals 205 and 434, and will be leaving Aave this July. He refuted the "extreme estimates" of the bad debt, stating that the actual figure is "far lower than that number," and urged users to withdraw their WETH from Aave V3 to mitigate risk. He added, "This incident will effectively test Umbrella," as if it were a stress test rather than a real loss of principal for users. That day, AAVE tokens fell 10.27%, closing at $105.73. This occurred while the scale of bad debts was still uncertain and a large number of WETH depositors were awaiting the Umbrella settlement deadline. The 0xQuit post was widely shared on the night of the attack. Many of those who posted it were Aave WETH depositors. Before sharing it, they read those lines repeatedly. "After Umbrella settlement, normal deposits should be able to withdraw partially." How much is "partial"? What does "normal" mean? And what does the word "should" mean? The last sentence from 0xQuit was, "This is a huge blow to the DeFi vision." One aspect of the DeFi vision is: your assets, your rules, and no one making decisions for you that you don't know about. Those decisions were made over the past six months in proposals submitted to the governance forum. No hackers forced their way in, and no code vulnerability predetermined this outcome. It was a repeated pursuit of "efficiency," a repeated disregard for "signals," and a critical period of inactivity that collectively resulted in this bill. The cost of governance is ultimately paid by those who neither participated in governance nor knew that governance had ever occurred. The code operated as approved. The bill went to those who didn't participate in the approval process.

data.title: Iran closes Strait of Hormuz again; Trump calls emergency Situation Room meeting
data.description:
With only three days left of the two-week ceasefire between the US and Iran, cracks are appearing on multiple fronts simultaneously.

On April 18, less than 24 hours after Iran announced the opening of the Strait of Hormuz, the Iranian military announced the reclosure of the strait. On the same day, Israel launched an attack in southern Lebanon, prompting Trump to convene an emergency White House Situation Room meeting.

According to media reports, a senior US official warned that if no breakthrough is achieved in the short term, the war could resume within days. Just the day before, Trump had stated that an agreement could be reached "within a day or two."

According to media reports, a senior US official warned that if no breakthrough is achieved in the short term, the war could resume within days. Just the day before, Trump had claimed that an agreement could be reached "within a day or two."

According to CCTV International News, the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy announced on the evening of the 18th local time that the Strait of Hormuz would be closed immediately until the US lifted its blockade of Iranian ships and ports due to the US violation of the ceasefire agreement. The Revolutionary Guard warned all ships in the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman not to leave their anchorages, and that any approach to the strait "would be considered cooperation with the enemy, and violating vessels would become targets." The statement also said, "The US president's remarks about the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf are completely unreliable." Just a day earlier, Iran had announced the opening of the strait to merchant ships sailing on designated routes. Trump immediately posted a "thank you" message, but then changed his tone, stating that "the maritime blockade against Iran remains in effect." Iran immediately accused the US of "maritime piracy." Iranian presidential spokesman Tabatabae stated on the 18th, "Due to the other side's repeated breaches of trust and its use of this significant concession for propaganda, the strait has been blocked again." After the strait was closed, chaos quickly ensued. According to media reports, the British Navy stated that an oil tanker was approached and fired upon by Revolutionary Guard speedboats near the coast of Oman, but the crew was safe; another container ship was hit by an unidentified projectile, damaging some containers. Several oil tankers attempting to pass through the strait turned back that day. However, some ships still attempted to seize the opening window from the previous day—a supertanker fully loaded with Qatari and Saudi crude oil, FPMC C Lord, transited the waters south of Iran's Larak Island towards the UAE, and several LNG carriers and oil tankers also headed towards the strait. Media analysis suggests that this reversal may expose the divide between hardliners in the Iranian military and the diplomatic system—the diplomacy's goodwill was quickly overturned by the military, reflecting the complexity and difficulty of the negotiations. Iran's newly appointed Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, stated in a declaration for National Army Day that day that "the navy is ready to make the enemy taste the bitter fruit of new defeats"; First Vice President Aref stated that Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz and will defend its rights "at the negotiating table or on the battlefield." The Strait of Hormuz is one of the world's most important energy transport routes. Before the outbreak of the US-Israel war against Iran, approximately one-fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas were transported through it. Trump convenes Situation Room meeting; US military prepares to board and seize Iranian ships. According to media reports, Trump convened an emergency meeting in the White House Situation Room on the morning of the 18th to discuss the Taiwan Strait crisis and negotiations with Iran. Vice President Vance, Secretary of State Rubio, Secretary of Defense Hergaces, and Treasury Secretary Bessenter were all present, along with White House Chief of Staff Wells, Special Envoy Witkov, CIA Director Ratcliffe, and Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Cain. Vance is expected to participate in the next round of negotiations with Iran. Military pressure is escalating simultaneously. According to Xinhua News Agency, citing the Wall Street Journal, the US military plans to board and seize oil tankers and merchant ships linked to Iran sailing in international waters in the coming days, expanding its reach beyond the Middle East. Xinhua also reported that Trump told reporters that the US is in talks with Iran, saying, "They want to block the Straits again, but Iran can't blackmail us. There will be news before the end of today." He had previously stated in an interview that Iran had agreed to an indefinite suspension of its nuclear program, and that "most of the key terms" had been finalized. However, he also threatened to resume bombing once the ceasefire expires. "Maybe I won't extend the ceasefire, so the blockade will continue. Unfortunately, we'll have to start throwing bombs again." The Lebanon-Israel ceasefire has been breached, and Hezbollah has issued a warning in response. The ceasefire agreement between Lebanon and Israel has already shown clear cracks after only one day in effect. According to CCTV News, the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) stated on the 18th that they had eliminated several Hezbollah members south of the Yellow Line in southern Lebanon within the past 24 hours, citing their violation of the ceasefire agreement and their "immediate threat." The IDF used artillery to support ground operations, destroying some Hezbollah infrastructure, and emphasized that the self-defense operation was "not bound by the ceasefire agreement." French President Macron confirmed on the same day that a French soldier was killed in an attack on UN peacekeeping forces in Lebanon, implying that Hezbollah should be held responsible. According to Xinhua News Agency, Hezbollah leader Qassem Soleimani issued a statement that day, saying he would respond to any violation of the ceasefire agreement, emphasizing that the ceasefire must be observed by both sides. The Lebanese military has previously recorded several attacks by the IDF, and some villages are still under intermittent shelling. The ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel, announced by Trump on April 16, will last for 10 days. There is a connection between the ceasefire and Iran's opening of the Strait of Hormuz—the simultaneous cracking of both lines has rapidly cooled previously escalating expectations for peace. The negotiation window is narrowing, and core differences remain unresolved. Despite the deteriorating situation, negotiations are still ongoing. According to Xinhua News Agency, Pakistani Army Chief of Staff Munir traveled to Tehran this week to mediate, and the US put forward new proposals. Iran's Supreme National Security Council stated that it is reviewing the proposals but has not yet responded. Media reports indicate that Trump has spoken with Munir and Iranian officials at least once. Media analysis suggests that the situation in the Taiwan Strait suddenly reversed precisely after the two sides narrowed their differences on uranium enrichment. Enriched uranium is a core bargaining chip in the negotiations. These materials were reportedly buried underground after the US and Israel bombed Iranian nuclear facilities during the 12-Day War last year, and their ownership is crucial to any agreement. Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Bagheri took a hard line: "Enriched uranium is as sacred to us as Iranian territory and will not be transferred anywhere under any circumstances." According to media reports, one of the proposed solutions is for the US to release $20 billion in frozen Iranian funds in exchange for Tehran handing over its enriched uranium stockpile, but Trump has repeatedly said "no" to this. The market has already priced in the expectation of peace. Brent crude oil plunged 9% to around $90 a barrel last Friday, erasing most of its gains since the outbreak of war; the spot benchmark dated Brent fell below $100 for the first time since March 11, while the stock market continued its rebound during the same period. But Bloomberg Economics analysts poured cold water on the idea in a report: "While an agreement seems within reach, it is unlikely to bring about comprehensive or lasting peace. We assess that any agreement will be limited and fragile."

data.title: Jensen Huang: Are top AI companies moving away from CUDA? "Your premise is wrong."
data.description:
For nearly two hours, Huang Renxun directly answered various questions about how Nvidia's market capitalization soared to $4 trillion during the era of big data.

Huang Renxun's new interview on "Silicon Valley's Most Popular Podcast" is packed with information.

Within half a day of its release, the video has already garnered over 100,000 views on YouTube alone.

Netizens also commented: It's rare to see Huang Renxun so excited.

TL;DR version, key points here:

  • Input is electronics, output is tokens, and in between is Nvidia.

  • AI will not make software cheap or homogenized; the widespread adoption of agents will rapidly increase tool deployment rates, leading to faster growth.

  • TPU poses no threat to Nvidia.

  • TPU poses no threat to Nvidia.

    • Nvidia GPUs make the invention of new algorithms easier. Nvidia's failure to invest in OpenAI and others earlier was a "misconception" and also "unavoidable." Nvidia's philosophy is "do as little as possible, but do what is necessary." Nvidia never prioritizes GPUs over funding. Even without deep learning, Nvidia will still focus on accelerating computing. More details are provided in the full transcript (including Huang's fierce rebuttal). Nvidia's competitive advantage (Question from podcast host Dwarkesh Patel, abbreviated as Q): Q: Software companies are experiencing a valuation crash because people believe AI will make software cheap. One potentially naive viewpoint is that Nvidia fundamentally makes software, while the manufacturing is done by others. If software becomes cheap, won't Nvidia lose its competitive advantage? Jensen Huang: Ultimately, there has to be something to convert electronics into tokens. This conversion from electronics to tokens, and the process of time making tokens more valuable, is very difficult to make homogeneous and cheap. The journey from electronics to tokens is incredible. The art, engineering, science, and invention invested in making one token more valuable than another are obvious. We are witnessing this process in real time, and everything involved is far from fully understood; this journey is far from over. I'm actually skeptical about whether your assumption will come true. Of course, we will make this process more efficient. Your question perfectly encapsulates Nvidia's operating philosophy: Input is electronics, output is tokens. In between lies Nvidia. Our job is to maximize this transformative capability by putting in the necessary effort while intervening as little as possible. "Intervening as little as possible" means delegating tasks not essential to us to our partners, making them part of the ecosystem. Today, Nvidia boasts the largest partner ecosystem, encompassing the entire supply chain, all computer companies, application developers, and model makers. Think of AI as a five-layer cake; our ecosystem covers every layer. We try to do as little as possible, but the part we have to do has proven incredibly challenging. I don't believe this part will become homogenized. In fact, I don't believe software companies or tool developers will lose their competitive advantage… most software companies today are tool developers. For example, Excel is a tool, PowerPoint is a tool, Cadence makes tools, and Synopsys also makes tools. My view is exactly the opposite; I believe the number of agents will grow exponentially, and the number of users of these tools will also grow exponentially. The deployment of these tools is likely to surge. Today, we are limited by the number of engineers. But in the future, there will be a large number of agents supporting engineers, exploring the design space in ways never seen before, and the tools we use today will not be discarded. I believe the proliferation of tools will lead to rapid growth for software companies. The reason it hasn't fully happened yet is because agents are not efficient enough in using these tools. Either these companies will build their own agents, or the agents will evolve to be able to use these tools efficiently. I think the two will combine. Q: In your recent filings, Nvidia's procurement commitments in areas such as foundries, memory, and packaging are close to $100 billion. SemiAnalysis says your related procurement commitments actually reach $250 billion. One explanation is that Nvidia's moat lies in securing the supply chain for these scarce components in advance. Is this Nvidia's biggest moat in the next few years? Jensen Huang: This is something we can do, but it's difficult for others. We've made huge commitments upstream. Some commitments are explicit, such as the procurement contracts you mentioned. Others are implicit, such as many upstream investments driven by our supply chain partners. I would say to the CEOs of these manufacturers, "Let me tell you how big this industry will be, let me explain why, let me work with you to project the future, and show you what I see." In this way, I constantly communicate with, motivate, and reach agreements with CEOs in different upstream industries. This makes them willing to invest. Why are they willing to invest in me, and not others? Because they know I have the ability to take over their supply and sell it through my downstream channels. The truth is, Nvidia's downstream supply chain and demand are enormous, and they're willing to invest in it. If you've attended GTC, you'll be amazed by its scale and the number of attendees. It's a complete 360-degree panorama, bringing together the entire universe of artificial intelligence. People come together because they need to understand each other. I bring them together so that downstream can see upstream, and upstream can see downstream, while also allowing everyone to see the latest developments in AI. Most importantly, they can also meet with AI-native companies and startups and witness firsthand everything I tell them. I spend a significant amount of time directly or indirectly communicating about future opportunities to our supply chain, partners, and ecosystem. People always say, "Jensen Huang, most of your keynote speeches are just one presentation after another." In fact, there's always a part of my presentations that's quite "torturous," almost like a lecture. I did it on purpose. I needed to make sure our entire supply chain—both upstream and downstream—understood the changes happening, why they were happening, when they were happening, and how large they were, and could reason about them as systematically as I could. Returning to the issue of the moat, we are preparing for the future—if our business reaches a trillion dollars in the next few years, our supply chain is ready for it. Without our market coverage and business drivers… just as cash flow has its liquidity, so does the supply chain. Without sufficiently frequent business liquidity, no one would be willing to build a supply chain to support architectural expansion. We can withstand such a large expansion because our downstream demand is enormous. And everyone has witnessed that. This allows us to accomplish everything we do at our current scale. Q: I'd like to understand more specifically whether the upstream is keeping up with demand. Over the past few years, your annual revenue has doubled, and the floating-point operations per second (flops) you provide globally has more than tripled.

    Jensen Huang: Doubling revenue at this scale is indeed incredible.

    Q: That's true. But when we talk about logic chips, you are TSMC's largest customer for the N3 node, and also one of the major customers for the N2 node. According to SemiAnalysis, AI will account for 60% of N3 capacity this year, and will reach 86% next year. In this situation, if you already occupy the majority of capacity, how do you continue to achieve double-digit growth? Is there a situation where the growth rate of AI computing power must slow down due to upstream factors? Have you seen a way to circumvent this problem? How do we double the capacity of the wafer fab every year?

    Jensen Huang: To some extent, instantaneous demand has exceeded the total supply of both upstream and downstream globally. At any given moment, we may be limited by the number of "plumbers," and that certainly happens. Q: So next year's GTC conference should invite plumbers to attend (laughs). Jensen Huang: That's a good idea (laughs). But having demand that exceeds industry supply capacity is a good thing. Obviously, the opposite is not good. If the gap between supply and demand is too large, the industry will quickly converge on the gap. For example, you'll find that almost no one talks about CoWoS packaging technology anymore. Q: Why? Jensen Huang: The reason is that the industry has invested heavily in this over the past two years, and the scale has even doubled several times. We are currently in a pretty good position in this regard. TSMC now knows that the supply capacity of CoWoS must keep up with the demand for logic chips and memory. They are expanding CoWoS and future packaging technologies to a level that is in sync with the development of logic chips. This is fantastic because for a time, CoWoS and HBM memory technologies were considered "specialty technologies." But now they've become mainstream computing technologies. Of course, now we can influence the supply chain much more broadly. I was saying a lot of what I'm saying now back in the early days of the AI ​​revolution. At that time, some people believed and invested, like Sanjay and his team at Micron. I was very impressed with that meeting; I clearly explained why things were happening this way and my predictions for the future. And they really doubled down, partnering with us in LPDDR and HBM memory. This undoubtedly brought tremendous growth to their companies. Some people arrived a little later, but they're all here now. We pay close attention to every bottleneck. Now we anticipate these bottlenecks years in advance. For example, our investments in collaborations with Lumentum, Coherent, and the silicon photonics ecosystem over the past few years have truly reshaped the supply chain. We've built a complete supply chain around TSMC, collaborated with them on the COUPE project, invented a bunch of new technologies, and licensed patents to the supply chain to keep it open. We help our partners expand capacity through new technologies, new workflows, new testing equipment, and investments. You can see that we're trying to ensure the supply chain can support this kind of large-scale development through ecosystem building. Q: It seems some bottlenecks are easier to solve than others. Scaling CoWoS to a larger scale might be relatively easy—Huang Renxun: By the way, I picked the most difficult example. Q: Which one? Huang Renxun: Plumbers and electricians. This is also one of the things that worries me about some "doomsday theorists" who always describe jobs being terminated and positions disappearing. If we advise people not to become software engineers, we will face a shortage of software engineers. Similarly, ten years ago, some pessimists made similar predictions, saying, "Whatever you do, don't become a radiologist." You can still find videos online saying that the profession of radiologist will be the first to disappear. But what has happened? We are precisely short of radiologists. Q: Returning to the earlier question about some bottlenecks that are easier to solve. How to manufacture twice the number of logic chips per year? The expansion of logic chips and memory chips is limited by extreme ultraviolet lithography (EUV). How to achieve twice the growth per year? Jensen Huang: This can be scaled up quickly. These are not difficult; you just need to demand signals. Once you can make one, you can make ten, then a million. All of this is easily replicated. Q: How deeply will you get involved? Will you communicate with ASML and tell them, "Look at the demand three years from now. To achieve $2 trillion in revenue for Nvidia annually, we need more EUV lithography machines." Jensen Huang: Some I have to speak directly to, some are achieved indirectly. For example, if I convince TSMC, ASML will naturally be convinced. The key is that we must consider critical bottlenecks. But once TSMC is convinced, you will see enough EUV equipment within a few years. My view is that no bottleneck will last more than two or three years. At the same time, we are also making great progress in improving computing efficiency. For example, the efficiency improvement from Hopper to Blackwell architecture is 30-50 times. Because of the flexibility of CUDA, we are able to develop entirely new algorithms. In addition, we are increasing production capacity while improving computing efficiency. These issues are not so worrying to me. The real risks come from downstream issues, such as policies restricting energy expansion. You can't build an industry without energy; you can't build a new manufacturing company without energy. We want to reshape American industry. We want to bring back chip manufacturing, computer manufacturing, and packaging processes; we want to build new things, like electric cars and robots; we want to build AI factories. But you can't do all that without energy, and these problems will take a long time to solve. In contrast, the chip production capacity issue can be resolved in just 2-3 years. CoWoS capacity expansion will also take 2-3 years. Q: That's interesting. I feel like the guests I invite sometimes express completely opposite views. In those cases, I lack the technical knowledge to judge. Jensen Huang: The good news is you're talking to an expert right now (laughs). TPUs are not a threat; Nvidia is "redefining computing." Q: I have a question about competitors. Two of the world's top three AI models—Claude and Gemini—are trained on TPUs. What does this mean for Nvidia's future? Jensen Huang: What we're building is very different from TPUs. Nvidia is building Accelerated Computing, not just a Tensor Processing Unit (TPU). Accelerated Computing can be used for a wide range of purposes: molecular dynamics, quantum chromodynamics, data processing, data frames, structured and unstructured data. It's also used in fluid dynamics and particle physics. Furthermore, we use it for AI computing. Accelerated Computing is much more versatile. While everyone is talking about AI today, and AI is indeed very important and has a profound impact, the scope of computing is far broader than that. Nvidia has redefined computing, transitioning from general-purpose computing to accelerated computing. Our market reach is far greater than any TPU or ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) can achieve. We are the only company that can accelerate a wide range of applications. We have a huge ecosystem, so various frameworks and algorithms can run on Nvidia platforms. Furthermore, most self-built systems are not designed for easy operation by others. Our systems are ubiquitous, including on Google, Amazon, Azure, and OCI (Oracle Cloud Infrastructure), because anyone can use and operate our systems. If you want to operate this computing power through leasing, you'd better have a large, multi-industry customer ecosystem to absorb these resources. If you want to use it yourself, we can obviously help you operate these computing systems, for example, we support Elon Musk's xAI. Because we can support operators from any company and any industry, you can use it to build supercomputers specifically for scientific research and drug discovery, like the Eli Lilly. We can help them operate their own supercomputers to accelerate the entire diverse process of drug discovery and bioscience. There are a large number of applications that TPUs cannot cover. NVIDIA has built CUDA into an excellent tensor processing unit, but it can also handle the entire lifecycle of data processing, computing, AI, etc. Our market opportunity is broader and has a wider reach. Because we support all types of applications in the world, you can build an NVIDIA system anywhere and be sure there will be customer demand. This is a completely different concept. Q: Now for a long question. Your revenue is phenomenal, and this money doesn't come from pharmaceuticals or quantum computing. The reason you can have $60 billion in revenue per quarter is because AI is an unprecedented technology, and its growth rate is also unprecedented. So the question is, for artificial intelligence, what is the most suitable option? I'm not familiar with the details, but when I talked to my AI researcher friends, they said, "Look at the TPU, it's a large array of matrix multiplications, perfect for performing matrix multiplications, while GPUs are very flexible. GPUs excel when there are many branches or irregular memory accesses." But what is the essence of AI? It's just performing predictable matrix multiplications over and over again. You don't need to waste any chip area on warp schedulers or switching between threads and memory groups. And the TPU is indeed optimized for the main growth needs and use cases of AI computing today. I'd like to know your response to this. Jensen Huang: Matrix multiplication is indeed an important part of AI, but it's not everything. If you want to develop a new attention mechanism, decouple it in a different way, or invent a completely new architecture, such as a hybrid SSM (state-space model), you need a general-purpose programmable architecture. If you want to build a fusion of diffusion and autoregressive models, you also need a general-purpose programmable architecture. We can run everything you can imagine. This is our strength: **Our architecture makes it easy to invent new algorithms because it's a programmable system.** The ability to invent new algorithms is the real driving force behind the rapid advancements in AI. Devices like the TPU are also limited by Moore's Law, with a growth rate of approximately 25% per year. The only way to achieve a 10x or 100x leap is to fundamentally change the algorithms and the way we compute. This is NVIDIA's core strength. The reason we were able to achieve a 50x performance improvement from Hopper to Blackwell… when I first announced that Blackwell was 35x more energy efficient than Hopper, nobody believed me. Later, Dylan wrote an article pointing out that I was actually "deliberately conservative," and that it was actually 50x. This simply couldn't be achieved by relying solely on Moore's Law. Our approach to this problem is through new models, such as MoE, which parallelize, decouple, and distribute implementations within the computing system. Without CUDA support, developing such a new kernel would be virtually impossible. Our advantage lies in the programmability of NVIDIA's architecture, and also in our strong collaborative design capabilities. We can even offload some computation to the computing architecture, such as NVLink; or integrate it into the network, such as Spectrum-X. We can influence every aspect of the processor, system, architecture, library, and algorithm simultaneously. Without CUDA, I wouldn't even know where to begin developing. Q: This touches on an interesting question about the characteristics of NVIDIA's customer base. Currently, 60% of your revenue comes from the five major cloud service providers. In a different era, with different customers—for example, professors conducting experiments—they needed CUDA. They couldn't use other accelerators; they just needed to run PyTorch with CUDA and ensure everything was optimized smoothly. But these cloud service providers had the resources to write their own kernels. In fact, they had to do so to get that final 5% of performance needed for their specific architectures. Anthropic and Google have moved to their own accelerators, such as TPUs and Trainium. Even OpenAI, which uses NVIDIA GPUs, has developed tools like Triton because they need their own kernels. From CUDA C++ to cuBLAS and NCCL, they have a complete, independent stack that can be compiled to other accelerators. Given that most customers can and are actually building CUDA alternatives, is CUDA still the key reason why the cutting-edge AI field still chooses NVIDIA? Jensen Huang: CUDA is a rich ecosystem. If you want to develop software on any computer, choosing CUDA first is definitely a smart choice. Because the ecosystem is so rich, we support every development framework. If you want to create a custom kernel… for example, we have made huge contributions to Triton. Triton's backend incorporates a lot of NVIDIA technology. We are very happy to help each framework become perfect. There are many frameworks available, such as Triton, vLLM, SGLang, and many more emerging reinforcement learning frameworks like Verl and NeMo RL. The field of post-training and reinforcement learning is experiencing explosive growth. Therefore, if you're building on an architecture, CUDA is the wisest choice because you know the ecosystem is robust and reliable. You'll know that if something goes wrong, it's most likely in your code, not in the underlying massive amount of code. Don't forget, the amount of code you're dealing with when building these systems is enormous. When something doesn't work, is it you who's wrong, or is it the computer? You'll always hope it's you who's wrong and trust the computer's robustness. Of course, our own systems have problems, but they're deeply optimized, and you can at least build on that reliable foundation. That's the first point: the richness, programmability, and capabilities of the ecosystem. Secondly, if you're a developer, the most important thing when building anything is installing the foundation. You want your software to run on many other computers. You're not just building it for yourself, but for your team and even other teams. If you're a framework developer, NVIDIA's CUDA ecosystem is an invaluable treasure trove of hardware and software. Hundreds of millions of NVIDIA GPUs are deployed worldwide, on every cloud platform. A10, A100, H100, H200, various L-series and P-series devices—a vast array of types and forms. We're practically everywhere. This massive installed base means that once your software or model is developed, it can run anywhere in the world—an invaluable asset. Finally, our widespread adoption on cloud platforms truly makes us unique. If you're an AI company or developer unsure which cloud service provider to work with or where to run your system, NVIDIA's system covers it all—including running directly within your company. The richness of this ecosystem, the breadth of its installed base, and the flexibility of its deployment models make CUDA irreplaceable. Q: That makes sense. I'm interested in whether these advantages are still as important to your major customers. For users in most industries, this may be very important. But for customers who can actually build their own software stacks—these customers make up the bulk of your revenue, especially in a world where AI is becoming increasingly powerful… the question ultimately becomes: if hyperscale enterprises can write their own kernels instead of relying on CUDA, can Nvidia maintain its current profit margins? Jensen Huang: The number of engineers we have deployed in these AI labs is staggering. We continuously optimize their software stacks because no one understands the complexity and details of their architecture better than we do. These architectures aren't as "general-purpose" as CPUs. A CPU is like a Cadillac—it runs smoothly, its performance doesn't fluctuate wildly, and anyone can drive it well. But NVIDIA's GPUs and accelerators are more like Formula One race cars. I can imagine anyone driving these GPUs at 100 mph, but to truly push them to their limits requires extremely high expertise. We also use a lot of AI to optimize our existing kernel libraries. I'm pretty sure our expertise will remain indispensable to our collaborating AI labs for a long time to come. We often enable them to further optimize their software stacks, resulting in 1 to 2x performance improvements. Sometimes optimizing a specific kernel can directly improve performance by 2x or 3x. This improvement is crucial for customers running large numbers of Hopper or Blackwell devices because it directly increases the efficiency of the entire facility, consequently boosting customer revenue. Without a doubt, NVIDIA's computing software stack offers the best total cost of ownership (TCO) in the world. No single platform offers a higher performance-to-TCO ratio than ours. The benchmarks are there, and I encourage TPUs or Trainium to use InferenceMAX and MLPerf to demonstrate their supposedly amazing inference cost advantages, but nobody's willing to do it. From a first-principles perspective, this simply doesn't make sense. I think the reason we're so successful is simple: our Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) is outstanding. Secondly, you mentioned that 60% of our customers are from the top five cloud computing companies, but most of that business is actually directed towards external customers. They choose us because of our strong customer reach. We can bring them the best customers globally. These customers choose NVIDIA because of our unique breadth and versatility. I think the flywheel effect comes from several aspects: our installed base, the programmability of our architecture, the richness of our ecosystem, and the presence of a large number of AI companies. There are thousands of AI companies now. If you were one of these AI startups, which architecture would you choose? You would choose the most widely adopted architecture globally—that's us. You would also choose the architecture with the largest installed base—that's also us. And there's an architecture with a rich ecosystem—that's a unique advantage of NVIDIA. So, that's where the flywheel comes in. The core reasons for our success include: First, performance and cost advantages. Our performance per dollar is outstanding, resulting in the lowest cost for our customers. Second, energy efficiency advantages: our performance per watt is the highest globally. If a company builds a 1GW data center, that data center must be able to generate maximum revenue and as many tokens as possible, which directly translates into income. And we have the architecture with the most tokens per watt globally. Finally, if your goal is to rent out infrastructure, we have the most customers globally. Q: Interesting. I think the key issue is the market structure. Perhaps there exists a world with thousands of AI companies, each with roughly equal computing power. But from the perspective of the five major cloud service providers, those actually using these computing resources are Anthropic, OpenAI, and large foundational model labs capable of building their own accelerators.

    Huang Renxun: No, I think your assumption is wrong.

    Q: Maybe, but let me ask you a slightly different question.

    Huang Renxun: No, let me correct your assumption.

    Q: Okay. Let me ask you another question.

    Huang Renxun: But still, please make sure I correct this assumption. Because this is too important for AI, too important for the future of science, and too important for the future of the industry. This assumption… let me explain— Q: Let me finish the question first, then we can discuss this topic together. Jensen Huang: Okay. Q: If the metrics regarding price, performance, and performance per watt are true, what's your take on this? For example, Anthropic recently announced a multi-gigawatt TPU computing agreement with Broadcom and Google, where most of their computations are done via TPUs. Clearly, for Google, TPUs provide the primary computing resources. And from my observation, these large AI companies seem to have relied heavily on Nvidia for most of their computing resources… but not anymore. So, if these parameters are accurate on paper, what's your take on these companies still choosing other accelerators? Jensen Huang: Anthropic is a special case, not a trend. Would TPUs have grown without Anthropic? They're entirely driven by Anthropic. Would Trainium have grown without Anthropic? Also entirely driven by Anthropic. This isn't about a large number of ASIC opportunities, but rather the existence of only one Anthropic. Q: But the collaboration between OpenAI and AMD… they're developing their own Titan accelerator. Jensen Huang: Yes, but we can all acknowledge that OpenAI's main computing still relies on Nvidia. We're still collaborating extensively. I don't mind other companies trying different things. How will they know how good we are if they don't try these products? We also need to be reminded that we must continue to work hard to maintain our current position. There will always be exaggerated claims. But look at the number of ASIC projects that have been cancelled in the past. Making a product better than Nvidia isn't easy. It's not wise either. Of course, Nvidia will definitely have its shortcomings, but in terms of scale and speed, we are the only company that is driving significant technological leaps every single year—every single year.
