Q: I think their logic might be: "Hey, these products don't need to be better, as long as they're not 70% worse than Nvidia's," because buying from you means paying 70% profit. Jensen Huang: Don't forget, even ASICs have very high profit margins. Assuming Nvidia's profit margin is 70%, ASIC's profit margin is close to 65%. How much do you actually save? Q: You mean Broadcom? Jensen Huang: Yes. You always have to pay a company. From the data I'm familiar with, ASIC profit margins are extremely high. They themselves think so, and they're proud of their astonishing ASIC profit margins. A long time ago, we didn't have the capacity to do this. Back then, I didn't fully realize how difficult it is to build a foundational AI lab like OpenAI or Anthropic; they require huge investments from vendors. We couldn't provide billions of dollars in investment to get Anthropic to use our computing resources, but Google and AWS could. They invested heavily early on, eventually getting Anthropic to use their computing resources. We couldn't do that back then. My mistake was not recognizing that AI labs have no choice; venture capital firms will never invest $5 billion to $10 billion in a lab. But even if I had understood that, I don't think we could have done it back then. Fortunately, I won't make the same mistake again. I'm happy to invest in OpenAI and help them scale. I'm also glad that when Anthropic approached us, we were able to invest in and support them. We couldn't do it in the past, and if I could do it again—if Nvidia had the scale we have today—I would be more than happy to do it. Why doesn't Nvidia become a super cloud service provider? Q: That's very interesting. For years, Nvidia has been the most profitable company in the AI ​​field. Now you're investing; reportedly, you've invested as much as $30 billion in OpenAI and $10 billion in Anthropic. And now, their valuations have grown significantly, and I believe they will continue to grow. So, over the years, you've been providing computing power to these companies, and you can see where they're headed. A few years ago, even just a year ago, their valuations were only a tenth of what they are now, and you had plenty of cash on hand then. Logically, one possibility is that Nvidia could have built its own basic research lab and made huge investments to make all of this possible, or completed the deal you're doing now much earlier, before the high valuation. I'm curious, why didn't you do it sooner?

    Huang Renxun: We did it as soon as we could. If the conditions had been available earlier, I would have done it even earlier. But when Anthropic needed us to do it, we didn't have the conditions. It wasn't a reasonable choice for us at the time.

    Q: Why? Was it because of funding?

    Huang Renxun: Yes, it was about the scale of the investment. At that time, we had never invested in external companies, especially not on such a large scale. We didn't realize it was necessary. I always thought they could go to venture capital like other companies. But the goals they wanted to achieve couldn't be achieved through venture capital. OpenAI's goals couldn't be achieved through venture capital either. I realize that now, but I didn't understand it then. But that's also their cleverness. They realized early on that they had to do this. I'm glad they made that choice back then. Although it forced Anthropic to find someone else, I'm still happy for them to exist. Anthropic's existence is a good thing for the world, and I'm genuinely happy about it. Q: Of course, you're still making a lot of money, and more every quarter. Jensen Huang: Even so, there can still be regrets. Q: The question remains—now that you have a lot of money on hand and keep making more, what should you do with that money? One answer is that an ecosystem of intermediaries is emerging, enabling these research labs to convert financial expenditures into operational expenses so that these labs can rent computing resources. Chips are very expensive, but they generate tremendous value over their lifecycle as AI models become increasingly powerful. Nvidia has ample funds to cover such capital expenditures. In fact, it's reported that you've provided up to $6.3 billion in support for CoreWeave and invested $2 billion in it. So why doesn't Nvidia become a cloud service provider itself and lease these computing resources? Jensen Huang: This is a matter of company philosophy. Nvidia should do "what must be done, but as little as possible." This means that the work we're doing to build computing platforms is this: if we don't do it, I genuinely believe no one else will. If we didn't build NVLink the way we do it now, if we didn't build the entire technology stack the way we do it now, if we didn't build the entire ecosystem the way we do it now, if we didn't persist in building CUDA for the past 20 years—most of that time was unprofitable—if we didn't do all of this, no one else will. If we hadn't created all the CUDA-X libraries, making them domain-specific… Over a decade ago, we started focusing on domain-specific libraries. We realized that if we hadn't created these libraries, whether for ray tracing, image generation, or early AI development, then technologies like data processing, structured data processing, and vector data processing wouldn't exist. We even created a library called cuLitho for computational lithography. If we hadn't created it, no one else would have. So, if we hadn't done this work, accelerated computing wouldn't be where it is today. So, this is something we absolutely must do. We should go all out and do everything we can to accomplish it. However, there are many cloud service providers in the world; if we don't do it, someone else will. NVIDIA's philosophy is to do "what must be done, but less is more," and everything revolves around this core principle. Regarding cloud services, if we didn't support the existence of "new cloud service providers" like CoreWeave, then these AI cloud companies wouldn't exist. Without our support, CoreWeave wouldn't exist. Without our support, Nscale wouldn't be where it is today. Without our support, Nebius wouldn't be where it is today. And now, they're thriving.

    Q: Why don't you pick the winners?

    Huang Renxun: First, it's not our responsibility. Second, when Nvidia was founded, there were 60 companies doing 3D graphics. Finally, only we survived. But if you had asked which of those 60 companies would have survived, Nvidia would likely have been listed as the least likely.

    Back then, Nvidia's graphics architecture was completely wrong. Not a little wrong, but completely wrong.

    We designed an architecture that developers simply couldn't support. It could never succeed. We reasoned based on correct first principles, but ultimately arrived at the wrong solution.

    Back then, everyone would have excluded us from the competition. But look at where we are now. Therefore, I know I need to be humble enough. Don't pick winners. Either let them compete among themselves, or support everyone. Q: There's something I don't understand. You said Nvidia doesn't prioritize supporting new cloud service companies, but then listed many new cloud service companies and said, "They wouldn't exist without Nvidia's support." How are these two statements compatible? Jensen Huang: First, they need to have the will to survive and actively seek our help. When they desire to exist and have their own business plans, expertise, and passion—obviously they must possess some capabilities. But ultimately, they need some investment to establish themselves, and we will support them. The sooner we get their flywheel effect going, the better. Your question is, "Do we want to be financial investors?" The answer is no. Fundraising is someone else's domain; we prefer to partner with everyone involved in fundraising rather than become financiers ourselves. Our goal is to focus on what we do best, keeping our business model as simple as possible while supporting our ecosystem. For example, when OpenAI needed up to $30 billion in investment, we stepped in to help them. The world needs them. The world craves their presence, and I want them to exist. They have strong growth momentum right now. We will support them and help them scale. We will make this investment because they need us. But we're not trying to do "as much as possible," but "as little as possible." Q: This question might seem obvious, but we've been facing a GPU shortage for years, and now, with advancements in models, the supply-demand gap seems even larger. Jensen Huang: That's right, GPUs are still in short supply. Q: Yes. Nvidia allocates scarce resources in a unique way, not simply on a highest bidder basis, but more on the premise that "we want these new cloud service companies to exist," thus allocating some resources to companies like CoreWeave, Crusoe, and Lambda. Why does Nvidia take this approach? Do you agree with this market description? Jensen Huang: No, no, your premise is wrong. We are very cautious about these things. First, if you don't place an order, then no amount of discussion will help. We can't do anything until we receive an order. So the first step is: we work with everyone to do demand forecasting, because these things take a long time to produce, and data center construction also takes a long time. We coordinate supply and demand through forecasting, that's the first step. Secondly, we do demand forecasting with as many people as possible, but ultimately it comes down to actual orders. What can we do if, for some reason, you don't place an order? After a certain point, it's on a "first-come, first-served" basis. However, if your data center isn't ready, or some components aren't available to get it up and running, we might prioritize other customers. This is simply to maximize the utilization of our own factory capacity; we might make some of these adjustments. Otherwise, the priority is "first-come, first-served." You need to place an order. If you don't, there's really nothing you can do. Of course, this could turn into some stories, like the previous reports about Larry Page, Elon Musk, and I having dinner together, asking for GPUs. That's completely untrue. We did have dinner together, and it was a very pleasant dinner. But they absolutely didn't beg for GPUs. They just placed an order. Once the order is placed, we do our best to meet their needs. It's not complicated. Q: Okay, so it sounds like there's a queue. If your data center is ready and the order is placed at a certain time, then you get delivered in order. But it still doesn't sound like the highest bidder gets priority. Why take this strategy? Huang: We never do that. Q: Okay. Huang: We never do. Q: Why not sell to the highest bidder? Huang: Because that's bad business practice. You set a price and then let people decide whether to buy. I know other companies in the chip industry adjust prices when demand is high, but we don't. We've never done that. You can rely on us. I'd rather be the foundation of the industry, without customers having to guess. If we give you a quote, that's the final price. If demand skyrockets, let it skyrocket. Q: On the other hand, this is also why you have such a good working relationship with TSMC, right? Jensen Huang: Yes, Nvidia and TSMC are about to celebrate their 30th anniversary. We haven't even signed a legal contract. Some things are generally about fairness; sometimes I benefit, sometimes I suffer. But overall, we have an excellent relationship. I can completely trust them and rely on them completely. What you can trust about Nvidia is this: every year, you can expect progress from us. This year it's Vera Rubin, next year it's Vera Rubin Ultra, then Feynman, and the year after that might be a new product yet to be named. Every year, we give you something to look forward to. Looking across the entire ASIC field, it's hard to find another team so consistently reducing per-chip costs by an order of magnitude every year while maintaining high productivity.

    Even without deep learning, Nvidia will still do accelerated computing

    Q: An interesting question. Suppose you already occupy most of TSMC's 3nm process capacity, and will continue to do so at the 2nm node. Do you think that, given the enormous demand for AI and the inability of cutting-edge capacity to meet it, you could go back and utilize the remaining capacity of older process nodes like 7nm, for example, to manufacture a chip based on the Hopper or Ampere architecture, but combined with existing numerical optimization techniques and the other improvements you mentioned? Do you think we will see this situation before 2030?

    Huang Renxun: There's no need for that. The reason is that each generation of architecture relies on more than just transistor process technology. Engineering design, packaging, stacking, numerical optimization, and various improvements in system architecture all play a significant role. If we encounter insufficient production capacity, we have to revert to older process nodes and redesign the chip... The R&D investment required is something no one can afford. We can afford the investment to move forward, but we can't afford the cost of going back. Of course, if the situation is... let's do a thought experiment: if one day we conclude, "We can no longer obtain more cutting-edge production capacity," if that day really comes, I will certainly immediately choose to go back to using the 7nm process. Q: Someone raised a question: why doesn't Nvidia simultaneously develop multiple chip projects using different architectures? For example, you can develop wafer-level chips like Cerebras, or large packages like Dojo, or even a design without CUDA at all. You have enough resources and engineering talent to carry out these projects in parallel. So why put all your bets in one basket? Jensen Huang: Oh, we can do that. But the problem is, we haven't found a better idea. We could try these things, but they weren't any better. We tested all of them in the simulator, and the conclusion was clear: they were worse. So we won't do them. The project we're currently focusing on is exactly what we want to do. Of course, if the task categories change significantly—I'm not referring to algorithms, but rather a real change in task requirements, depending on the market landscape—then we might decide to add some other accelerators. For example, we recently introduced Groq and integrated it into the CUDA ecosystem. We did this because the value of tokens is incredibly high now, and this allows us to set different prices for them. A few years ago, tokens were either free or inexpensive. But now, our customers are more diverse, and they need different performance levels. For example, our software engineers, if I can provide a token with faster response times, making them more efficient than they are now, I'm willing to pay for it. This market is relatively recent. I think we can now segment the market based on response time. This is why we decided to expand the Pareto frontier and create an inference segment with faster response times, despite its lower throughput. Previously, increasing throughput had been a greater priority. But we believe there might be a future scenario where even with lower throughput, it makes sense due to a higher average selling price (ASP). That's why we did it. But overall, from an architectural perspective, if I had more resources, I would invest them in Nvidia's existing architecture. Q: I find this idea of ​​"high-premium tokens" and inference market segmentation very interesting. Jensen Huang: Yes, further market segmentation. Q: Okay, one last question. What would Nvidia be doing now if the deep learning revolution had never happened? Jensen Huang: Accelerating Computing – What We've Always Been Doing. We believe Moore's Law is slowing down… General-purpose computing performs well in many areas, but not so well in many computational tasks. Therefore, we combine an architecture called GPU with the CPU to accelerate the CPU's computational load. Different code kernels or algorithms can be offloaded to run on our GPUs. The result is that you can speed up an application by 100x, 200x. Where can this performance be used? Obviously in engineering and science fields, such as physics, data processing, computer graphics, image generation, and so on. Even without AI today, Nvidia would still be a very large company. There's a very fundamental reason for this: the potential for further expansion of general-purpose computing power has essentially reached its limit. A more feasible approach is domain-specific accelerators. One of our earliest ventures was computer graphics, but there are many others, such as particle physics and fluid simulations, structured data processing, and various algorithms that benefit from CUDA technology. Our mission has always been to bring accelerated computing to the world, driving applications that general-purpose computing couldn't achieve, and helping to push the boundaries of science. Some early applications included molecular dynamics, seismic processing for energy exploration, image processing, and all aspects of computer graphics—areas where general-purpose computing was far too inefficient. Without AI, I would be deeply disappointed. But it is because of our advancements in computing technology that deep learning has become ubiquitous. We enable researchers, scientists, and students to conduct amazing scientific research with a PC or a GeForce graphics card. This commitment has never changed, not even a little bit. If you watch GTC, the opening section has nothing to do with AI. Computational lithography, quantum chemistry research, data processing—these topics are unrelated to AI, but they are still very important. I know AI is exciting, but many people are doing important work that doesn't involve AI, and these computational tasks are not limited to tensor computation.

data.title: The biggest DeFi heist of 2026 – hackers steal from Aave and then conveniently scam it as well.
data.description:
Author: Xiaobing, Deep Tide TechFlow

At 17:35 (UTC) on April 18th, a wallet laundered with Tornado Cash sent a cross-chain message to LayerZero's EndpointV2 contract.

The message's semantics were simple: a user on a certain chain wanted to transfer rsETH back to the Ethereum mainnet. LayerZero faithfully relayed the instruction according to the protocol design. The bridge contract deployed by Kelp DAO on the mainnet also faithfully executed the release as designed.

116,500 rsETH, worth approximately $292 million at the time, were transferred to an address controlled by the attacker in a single transaction.

The problem is, no one on the other chain had ever stored this rsETH. This "cross-chain request" was fabricated out of thin air; LayerZero believed it, and the Kelp bridge also believed it.

46 minutes later, Kelp's emergency multisignature was finally paused. By then, the attackers had completed the second half of their operation, staking the stolen, essentially unpegged rsETH into Aave V3 and borrowing approximately $236 million worth of wETH.

This is the largest DeFi theft so far in 2026, exceeding the Drift protocol's loss by several million dollars more than the North Korean-backed hack on April 1st. But what truly sends chills down the spines of the industry is not just the amount.

How the attack happened: Three bets between 17:35 and 18:28

Let's reconstruct the timeline.

17:35 UTC, the first successful bet.

The attacker invoked the `lzReceive` function on the LayerZero EndpointV2 contract, a wallet backed by Tornado Cash, to send a forged cross-chain data packet to Kelp's bridging contract. Contract verification passed, and 116,500 rsETH were released to the attacker's address. A single transaction. Clean. At 18:21 UTC, Kelp's emergency suspension of multisignature froze the rsETH core contract on the mainnet and multiple L2 servers. 46 minutes after the attack. At 18:26 and 18:28 UTC, the attacker launched two more attempts, each with a LayerZero data packet attempting to withdraw another 40,000 rsETH (approximately $100 million). Both attempts were reverted, and the contract was frozen, but the attacker was clearly still trying to abscond with the remaining liquidity. Nearly three hours passed between the initial success and Kelp's public statement. Kelp's first X post wasn't published until 8:10 PM UTC, and its wording was very restrained: Suspicious cross-chain activity involving rsETH has been discovered; the mainnet and multiple rsETH contracts on L2 have been suspended; and they are collaborating with LayerZero, Unichain, auditors, and external security experts on root cause analysis. However, ZachXBT, a blockchain detective, gave a conclusion even earlier than the official statement. He issued an alert on his Telegram channel before 3 PM ET, listing six wallet addresses related to the theft and pointing out that the attacking wallets had all prepared funds through Tornado Cash before the operation began. He didn't name the Kelp DAO, but on-chain analysts connected the addresses in just a few hours. This was a premeditated operation executed within minutes. The pre-charged, laundered wallets, the meticulously crafted cross-chain data packets, and the coordinated attack and Aave collateralized lending—every step was executed with perfect timing. After stealing, they also planned to scam. If it were just a simple bridging vulnerability, stealing 116,500 rsETH and running away, it would at most be considered a major incident in 2026. Kelp would absorb the loss, the community would digest it for a few days, and the industry would move forward. But the attackers clearly did the math. rsETH itself doesn't have ample secondary liquidity. Directly dumping $292 million into DEXs for a sell-off would result in slippage eating up a significant portion of the profits. A more elegant way to exit was to package this "newly acquired rsETH" as seemingly respectable collateral and borrow truly liquid assets through lending protocols. The attackers then took a second step: depositing the stolen rsETH into Aave V3 as collateral to borrow a large amount of wETH. Why was this step fatal? Because at that moment, the Aave contract was still calculating the collateral value based on the oracle price of rsETH, while the reserves in the bridge had been emptied. The underlying economic entity of this batch of rsETH no longer existed. The lending protocol was still issuing loans according to the "100% gold content" standard, but the collateral was now a worthless check. The result was that the attackers transferred the risk of liquidating their funds to Aave's wETH reserve pool. Aave V3's wETH reserves are currently absorbing bad debts. Solidity developers and auditor 0xQuit reminded depositors on X that the wETH pool has actually been damaged, and some withdrawals may only be restored after Aave's Umbrella backup module clears the deficit. The latest estimate for bad debt is in the range of $177 million, and this is only on the Ethereum mainnet side. A prophetic first major test. For veteran DeFi players, this feels familiar; Aave V2's Safety Module played a similar role during the Luna crash in 2022. But this time, it's Umbrella. Aave will launch a new generation backup system in late 2025 to replace the old Safety Module, and this event is the first major real-world stress test of Umbrella's automatic bad debt coverage mechanism. Umbrella's logic is straightforward: stake aWETH, aUSDC, GHO, and other aTokens into the corresponding Umbrella vault to earn extra incentives. However, when the corresponding asset pool incurs a deficit, this staked portion will be slashed proportionally to cover the shortfall. This design looks good on paper. In the first month of Aave v3.3's operation, the total pool deficit was approximately $400, corresponding to nearly $9.5 billion in outstanding loans—a negligible proportion. However, the $177 million in bad debt is on a completely different scale. For users who staked aWETH in Umbrella, this means they will soon truly feel the weight of "bearing the risk of slashing" for the first time. Aave's official statement is cautious: if bad debt occurs, Aave plans to use Umbrella assets to cover any financial shortfall. However, whether it can be fully covered, what the slashing ratio is, and how much principal stakers have lost—these questions will only be answered after the settlement process is complete. The Original Sin of Cross-Chain Bridges Even more disturbing is the identity of the stolen rsETH. rsETH is deployed on more than 20 networks, including Base, Arbitrum, Linea, Blast, Mantle, and Scroll, with cross-chain transactions handled by LayerZero's OFT standard. The rsETH in the emptied bridges is the reserve that supports all the "wrapped" versions of rsETH on these networks. This design sounds conventional at first glance: the mainnet vault holds a 1:1 reserve, and rsETH holders on L2 can theoretically cross back to the mainnet to redeem their rsETH at any time. But this mechanism relies on the premise that the vault actually has money. The vault is now 18% empty. Approximately 18% of Kelp's total rsETH circulating supply lost its corresponding reserves overnight. This creates a feedback loop: panic redemptions by L2 holders will transmit pressure to the unaffected Ethereum supply, potentially forcing Kelp to unstake its positions to meet withdrawal requests. Restaking isn't a simple matter of pressing a button. EigenLayer withdrawals have a delay, and underlying validators have queuing periods for exits. If L2 rsETH holders rush to the redemption window, Kelp may not have enough time to prepare the mainnet's repayment ammunition. This is a fundamental risk of the bridge reserve model: if the mainnet, this single reservoir, fails, the pressure in all downstream channels will collapse. Every rsETH holder on every L2 blockchain is now facing the same choice: run first, or trust Kelp to bail them out? Panic swept across the entire DeFi lending sector within hours. Aave V3 and V4's rsETH market was frozen, and new deposits and rsETH-based lending channels were shut down. SparkLend and Fluid followed suit, freezing the rsETH market. Ethena, while stating it had no rsETH exposure and maintained over 101% overcollateralization, still suspended its LayerZero OFT bridge from the Ethereum mainnet as a precaution, with an expected suspension of about six hours. This reaction is quite intriguing: even players without direct exposure are suspending LayerZero-related bridges. Lido Finance has suspended new deposits for its earnETH product (because the product contains rsETH exposure), while emphasizing that stETH and wstETH are unaffected and that the Lido core staking protocol is unrelated to this event. Upshift has suspended deposits and withdrawals for High Growth ETH and Kelp Gain vaults. This list is still growing. Deep Tide Commentary: The Road to DeFi Security is Long and Arduous. As of this writing, the root cause analysis of Kelp DAO is still ongoing. How much of the stolen rsETH can be recovered through negotiations with security teams or white-hat hackers? Can Aave's Umbrella withstand this bad debt? Will rsETH holders on L2 trigger a run? Can the prices of AAVE and rsETH stabilize before the end of the weekend? However, some problems have already emerged. For example, can LRT continue to be a suitable collateral for lending protocols? Liquid Restaking Tokens were the darlings of the Ethereum ecosystem in the last cycle. EigenLayer initiated the narrative of "earning multiple layers of returns with one ETH," and protocols like Kelp, ether.fi, and Puffer industrialized this narrative. The end result was that LRT was included in the collateral whitelist by major lending protocols as a structured asset. This decision was based on the assumption that LRT's anchoring mechanism was robust enough, and that the risks of multi-layered nesting of underlying assets could be fully modeled and isolated at the smart contract level. The Kelp incident, in just one afternoon, exposed a major flaw in this assumption. LRT's risks stem not only from its underlying smart contracts but also from its cross-chain distribution architecture; not only from a single protocol but also from every dependency it has with EigenLayer, LayerZero, and Aave. Each individual piece of the DeFi Lego set may appear safe individually, but when they are assembled, the risks are multiplicative, not additive. In the coming months, all lending protocols still using LRT as high-grade collateral will need to reassess their risk parameters. Supply caps will decrease, liquidation buffers will increase, and some protocols may be delisted. DeFi's moat has always been called "composability," but this incident reminds everyone that composability is a double-edged sword. The network effects you pride yourself on are amplifiers in the hands of attackers. This attacker had planned their exit strategy beforehand. They weren't just stealing; they were using DeFi's composability as a weapon. The tighter the dependencies between protocols and the richer the composability, the wider the attacker's attack surface, and the more financial tools they can utilize. DeFi security remains a long and arduous task.

data.title: Payward raises $200 million from 17 projects that have raised $443 million.
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

This week, a total of 17 blockchain startups completed funding rounds, raising a total of $443 million.

Largest Funding Round This Week

Payward raised $200 million from Deutsche Börse Group.

Payward is the parent company of Kraken and its related products, and its business covers crypto trading, custody, payments, lending, on-chain finance, and benchmark services. Its positioning is to provide infrastructure for an open, programmable global market, connecting crypto assets, tokenized assets, and traditional finance through a unified, interoperable technology stack.

The company states that its business covers 190+ jurisdictions and holds 100+ licenses and registrations, encompassing crypto, securities, derivatives, payments, remittances, banking, and lending. Claw Intelligence has completed a $3 million seed funding round, with investors including Castrum Capital, Titans Ventures, Super Labs, and Genesis Capital VC. Claw Intelligence is an AI-powered Web3 intelligence and trading platform focused on the BNB Chain. It integrates market data, on-chain signals, research analysis, and execution tools into a single interface, aiming to transform fragmented blockchain information into structured, actionable insights. Its chat-based and modular design allows users to more efficiently transition from analysis to trade execution. KieDex Raises $3.5 Million Seed Funding. KieDex is a crypto futures trading platform focused on the high-leverage perpetual contract market, employing a "trade-to-earn" model. Positioned as a fast and simple exchange, users can start trading with free USDT and earn KDX rewards through trading activities. The platform emphasizes speed, fairness, and long-term incentives, leaning more towards derivatives DeFi trading than general-purpose exchanges or infrastructure protocols. Mirage Raises Seed Funding (Amount Undisclosed). Mirage is a non-custodial privacy protocol for stablecoin transfers on Ethereum and other EVM-compatible networks. It enables privacy payments similar to regular on-chain transactions without pooling funds. Mirage focuses on fast settlement, compliant integration, and easy embedding into wallets, payment applications, custody services, and DeFi products. Its core goal is to achieve low-friction, high-availability, and broadly compatible "privacy digital dollar transfers." Nava Labs raised $8.3 million in seed funding. Nava is a middleware verification layer for AI Agent blockchain transactions. Before funds are transferred, it verifies that the Agent's on-chain execution conforms to its original instructions. Through arbitration verification, execution custody, and a dedicated coordination layer (Nava Chain), the system performs security, correctness, and policy compliance analysis on transactions. Its goal is to make AI-automated DeFi operations more reliable, auditable, and financially secure, making it more suitable for institutional scenarios. Pumpcade raised $5 million in seed funding. Pumpcade is a Solana-based platform for instantly creating and trading meme tokens. It simplifies the token issuance process, allowing users to quickly publish and list assets without complex technical skills. The platform is community-driven, promoting participation, discovery, and viral spread within the meme token ecosystem. SHARE has completed its seed round of funding (amount undisclosed). Share is a decentralized internet infrastructure network (DePIN) focused on solving connectivity issues in Africa and emerging markets. Through a distributed node network, it reduces fragmentation, high costs, and dependence on centralized service providers. The platform provides open, reliable, and low-cost internet access and data services. Its goal is to improve accessibility and drive the growth of the digital economy in underdeveloped regions. Other Funding Rounds Mindra AI completed a strategic funding round (amount undisclosed). Mindra AI is a decentralized Large Model (LLM) infrastructure network that builds a "Intelligence Grid." It supports dynamic inference routing, cross-chain agent collaboration, and introduces AI-Fi to financialize computing resources. Through DeAI technology, it enhances network intelligence, scalability, and user experience while reducing reliance on centralized AI providers. Spektr completed a $20 million Series A funding round. Spektr is an AI-driven compliance operations platform that helps banks, fintech, and Web3 companies automate KYC, KYB, customer onboarding, monitoring, remediation, document review, ownership structure analysis, and risk assessment. It combines configurable processes with AI agents, enabling teams to scale compliance capabilities and reduce manual costs. Slash has completed a $100 million Series C funding round. Slash is a vertically integrated digital banking platform built specifically for online businesses. It offers checking accounts, virtual/physical cards (with cashback), transfer services, and integration with mainstream accounting software. It supports multi-user, real-time account partitioning, and seamless management of crypto and fiat currencies, serving e-commerce, marketing, and crypto-native enterprises. ZenGo was acquired by eToro for $70 million. ZenGo is a self-custodied crypto wallet that uses MPC technology to eliminate mnemonic phrase risk. It supports 380+ assets and provides 3D facial recognition and triple authentication recovery mechanisms. Brix raised $5.5 million. Brix is ​​a DeFi platform that brings institutional-grade strategies on-chain through structured yield products. Its first asset, iTRY, is a token based on the Turkish Lira Money Market Fund, allowing users to participate in arbitrage trading that was previously limited to banks. BlockInvest raised $4.7 million. BlockInvest is a Web3 investment and asset management platform for financial institutions. It leverages blockchain and AI to tokenize assets and improve data quality and liquidity. Totalis raised $500,000. Totalis is an on-chain prediction market derivatives layer that supports the construction of parlays. It covers multiple sectors including geopolitics, crypto, and sports. Paxos Labs (Amplify) raised $12 million. Paxos Labs is an enterprise-grade on-chain financial platform incubated by Paxos. Its core product, Amplify, provides a unified SDK that supports the integration of yield, lending, and stablecoin issuance functions. OpenGradient raised $9.5 million in funding. OpenGradient is a decentralized AI model deployment and execution platform. It provides NeuroML (similar to the Solidity framework) and a Python SDK for connecting to model libraries and infrastructure. Fluent Labs raised $1 million in public funding. Fluent is an Ethereum Layer 2 network and development framework that integrates EVM, SVM, and Wasm into a unified execution environment. This "hybrid execution" supports seamless interoperability between smart contracts in different languages ​​(Solidity, Rust, etc.).

(CCTV) “We do not trust the enemy. Even if we sit here right now, war could break out—the armed forces are fully prepared for the battlefield,” Ghalibaf said in a speech broadcast on Iranian state television Sunday morning. While Iran is actively negotiating with the United States for a permanent ceasefire agreement, he stated that Iran is prepared to respond at any time if the enemy “makes a mistake.”

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called “Digital Gold” During Geopolitical Crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, gold prices rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of “digital gold” has never been supported by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-prone asset," capable of functioning effectively when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Encrypted apps are being removed en masse: Google announces the end of the era of unlicensed, unprotected apps.
data.description:
Recently, many exchanges, wallets, and payment projects in the industry have been discussing the same situation:

They anxiously sent the same screenshot of their backend (see below), and then asked almost the same question: "Attorney Shao, we suddenly received app removal warnings from app stores in several countries. Is this due to competitors, or is it a targeted attack by regulators?"

Frankly, neither. Don't be paranoid, and don't think this is just an "isolated incident." If you've also received this "final warning," or if your team is still complacent about its rapid overseas expansion, please recognize this harsh reality: Behind this is the fact that global app stores are being forced to become "regulatory assistants." In the past, regulators only controlled your cash flow and office location; now, they are directly cutting off your traffic sources. This isn't targeting any one company, but a global purge of app stores. Google Play has officially included "crypto licenses" in its listing rules. In fact, Google Play already included "crypto licenses" in its "Cryptocurrency Exchange and Software Wallet Policy." The core logic is extremely simple and brutal: You must provide a license for the country where your app provides trading or custody services. Can't produce one? Sorry, it will be removed from the app store upon expiration. Google has explicitly listed the requirements for several jurisdictions, such as: the EU requires CASP authorization under MiCA granted by the relevant national competent authority; the UK requires FCA registration; the UAE requires FSRA, VARA, or DFSA licenses; the US requires registration as a FinCEN MSB and obtaining state-level money transmitter qualification, or having federal/state chartered bank status; South Korea requires successfully submitting a VASP report to KoFIU… In the past, you might only have needed to deal with regulatory agencies, but now you also have to deal with Google's compliance review team. This wave of app removals is not a punishment, but an “entry blockade.” Many Web3 founders are still stuck in the mindset of the last cycle: “So what if I don’t have a license? At worst, I won’t set up an office locally, how can the regulators check me?” Brother, times have changed. While regulators might not be able to trace your app across borders for a while, a single email from Google can cripple your growth engine. This is especially true in emerging markets like Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and Latin America, where Android users are dominant. What does being removed from Google Play mean? New users won't be able to find or download it. Existing users can't update, leading to a deteriorating user experience. Expensive advertising conversion rates will drop to zero. Most critically, once your partner banks and payment gateways see your app is gone, their risk control alarms will sound, and they'll immediately terminate your partnerships. Regulatory penalties are a matter of law and fines, but having an app's internet connection cut off is a matter of life and death, directly severing its cash flow. Why the sudden crackdown? It's simple: because the global regulatory framework is complete, and platforms are no longer willing to take the blame for you. Previously, you could argue that "there's no licensing mechanism in the local area." Now? Global regulatory rules like Hong Kong's VATP, Europe's MiCA, and Singapore's PSA are almost all in place. The path has been paved for you; if you don't get the license, don't blame the platform for being uncooperative. Even Google and Apple are afraid of being dragged down. How many money laundering tools, scam wallets, and collapsed exchanges have appeared on app stores in the past few years? The platforms' attitude is now clear: Prove you're legitimate first, then I'll give you traffic. The old gray growth path of "launching globally first, building up user base first, and then fixing things when regulators come knocking" is completely unworkable. It's not just exchanges that are being targeted. Don't think you're safe just because you're not a CEX (centralized exchange). Based on recent cases we've encountered, custodial wallets, OTC deposit and withdrawal platforms, U-card projects, even if you're just making an app with an Earn feature, or the recently popular RWA platform, payment aggregators, etc., as long as your application involves "token custody" and "financial attributes," are all within the scope of this crackdown. In the next year or two, the industry will become extremely polarized. Licensed, legitimate players can openly acquire traffic and connect to bank channels; while unlicensed, guerrilla-style operators can only launch and delist daily, playing a cat-and-mouse game with regulators, and will eventually be slowly worn down. The Solution: Not Remedying After Being Taken Down, but Preparing for Licensing in Advance. At this point, anxiety won't solve the problem. There seem to be two paths on the table, but in reality, there's only one. Some say: "At worst, I'll just stop operating in those dozens of countries and remove those regions." This is called the "ostrich policy." In today's world where traffic dividends have been exhausted, actively abandoning any mainstream market is tantamount to handing over hard-won territory to competitors who have already laid the groundwork for compliance. This is a form of slow suicide. There is only one path: confront it head-on and achieve full compliance. Apply for the corresponding compliance licenses in the relevant countries/regions. This is the only long-term solution that allows you to stay on the table. But problems arise: the regulatory requirements of dozens of countries and various legal systems around the world vary greatly. Applying for a FinCEN MSB in the US, obtaining CASP certification under the MiCA framework in Europe, and facing stringent scrutiny in Hong Kong… For most Web3 projects lacking a large multinational legal team, this is practically an impossible task. This is why more and more peers, recently facing delisting warnings and forced into a corner, are turning to Mankiw. Applying for a global crypto license is never a simple matter of "filling out forms and translating." It's incredibly complex: Which country is the fastest to obtain a license for your business model? How do you get your UBO funding verification approved? How do you appoint local directors and anti-money laundering officers? Even a one-click exchange function in your app might trigger the red lines for another license? These practical issues won't be taught on the official website, and even general intermediaries don't understand them.

data.title: Canaan Creative increases its holdings by 15 BTC; Ethereum Foundation's Josh Stark resigns.
data.description:
Bitcoin

Arkham: Morgan Stanley Bitcoin ETF On-Chain Address Now Holds 1348 BTC

Blockchain analytics platform Arkham announced that it has identified the on-chain wallet address of Morgan Stanley Bitcoin Trust (MSBT), becoming the first platform to publicly identify the ETF's on-chain BTC holdings, allowing users to track fund inflows and outflows in real time. The Morgan Stanley Bitcoin ETF was listed on the NYSE Arca on April 8th, with Coinbase and BNY Mellon serving as custodians. According to Arkham's tracking data, it currently holds 1348 BTC, worth approximately $103.92 million.

ETH Zurich Foundation Core Member Josh Stark Announces Resignation

On April 17th, Josh Stark, a core figure at the Ethereum Foundation (EF), announced his resignation after five years in office, officially stepping down at the end of the month. Stark joined the Ethereum Foundation in 2019, initially working on a special projects team before rising to leadership positions. He worked with EF Chairman Aya Miyaguchi, Ethereum founder Vitalik Buterin, and co-executive directors Hsiao-Wei Wang and Bastian Aue, among other senior figures. He was one of the most prominent members of the Foundation, leading several major developments in Ethereum, including "The Merge," the upgrade from Proof-of-Work to Proof-of-Stake, and subsequent upgrades such as Dencun, Fusaka, and Pectra. During a leadership reshuffle at the Foundation last year, he was appointed as "Co-Manager" of the EF Board of Directors and recently became co-chair of the "Trillion-Dollar Security" initiative.

... Last month, Stark co-authored an Ethereum strategy blog post with Josh Rudolf and Julian Ma, outlining Ethereum's latest scaling direction and its relationship with the Layer 2 ecosystem. Stark stated, "I haven't planned for the future yet; I just want to rest and spend time with family and friends." This departure comes against the backdrop of ongoing changes within the Ethereum Foundation. Last year, the Foundation underwent a major leadership reshuffle, subsequently refocusing its strategic emphasis on Ethereum mainnet scaling and core cypherpunk values. Tomasz K. Stańczak also resigned as co-executive director at the end of February this year, after serving less than a year. On the same day, Trent Van Epps also announced his departure from EF to dedicate himself full-time to Protocol Guild, the independent funding organization for Ethereum core developers that he founded.

ETH/BTC ratio rebounds from 2026 lows, signaling an overall recovery in the crypto market

On April 15th, Ethereum recently strengthened relative to Bitcoin, with the ETH/BTC exchange rate rising to approximately 0.0313, the highest level in three months, but still far below the January peak. This improvement in the ratio is attributed to strong on-chain activity on Ethereum: a surge of 82% in new users in the quarter, record-high transaction volume, and a record-high total stablecoin supply of $180 billion. Analysts say that Ethereum must recover the 0.035 ETH/BTC level on the weekly close to indicate that the rotation of funds into Ethereum is sustainable, rather than a short-lived rebound; especially since the current Ethereum price is still more than 50% below its 52-week high.

BitMine increased its holdings by over 71,000 ETH last week, bringing its total holdings to over 4% of the total ETH supply. As of April 12th, Eastern Time, BitMine's total cryptocurrency, cash, and "Moonshot" holdings totaled $11.8 billion. BitMine held 4,874,858 ETH (an increase of 71,524 ETH from the previous week), representing 4.04% of the total ETH supply (120.7 million ETH). It also held 197 BTC, $200 million worth of Beast Industries shares, $85 million worth of Eightco Holdings (NASDAQ: ORBS), and $719 million in uncollateralized cash. As of April 13th, 2026, BitMine's total staked ETH was 3,334,637 ETH (equivalent to $7.4 billion at $2,206 per ETH). Other Projects

Singapore's Gulf Bank Launches Stablecoin Minting and Redemption Service

On April 17th, Singapore's Gulf Bank (SGB) announced the launch of a stablecoin minting and redemption service. Corporate and high-net-worth clients can directly convert between fiat currency and stablecoins through their SGB accounts, achieving 24/7 instant settlement without relying on traditional correspondent banking processes. This service is integrated into SGB's proprietary clearing network, SGB Net, supporting seamless on-chain and off-chain fund flows. Initially supporting USD Coin (USDC) with a minimum transaction amount of US$100,000, USDT, USDe (Ethena), and Global Dollar (USDG) will be added later.

... Polymarket Adds "Claude Developer Anthropic's Next Funding Round Deadline" On April 18th, Polymarket added a new event regarding the "Claude developer Anthropic's next funding round deadline." Currently, the probability of the funding round being completed by the end of June is tentatively reported as 22%, and by the end of December as 81%. The event's contractual rules state that if the designated company publicly and formally announces the completion of its next funding round by the specified date (Eastern Time), the market will be classified as "Yes." Otherwise, the market will be classified as "No." A qualifying announcement must clearly confirm the completion of the new funding round, which can be achieved through official announcements from the designated company (such as press releases) or its investors, regulatory documents, or consensus from credible media reports. Informal announcements, statements from anonymous sources, or leaks are not acceptable. If the designated company is unable to complete the new funding round due to acquisition, merger, or absorption by another entity, the market will be classified as "No." The primary source of settlement information for this market will be official announcements from designated companies, as well as official company documents such as SEC filings; however, credible consensus reports may also be used.

Payward has agreed to acquire crypto derivatives company Bitnomial for $550 million

Kraken's parent company, Payward, announced that it has agreed to acquire stock and crypto derivatives trading company Bitnomial for $550 million in a cash-and-stock deal. This transaction allows Payward to control a fully licensed U.S. cryptocurrency derivatives stack, thereby accelerating its expansion in regulated markets.

... Bitcoin mining company TeraWulf completes approximately $1.035 billion rights issue. Nasdaq-listed Bitcoin mining company TeraWulf announced the completion of its previously announced public offering of 54,510,000 ordinary shares at $19.00 per share, raising a total of $1,035,690,000. Morgan Stanley served as the lead underwriter for the offering, with Bank of America Securities, Citigroup, TD Cowen, and Wells Fargo Securities serving as joint underwriters. Citizens Capital Markets and Santander Bank served as co-underwriters, and Cantor Fitzgerald served as equity capital markets advisor. Charles Schwab CEO: Considering Entering the Prediction Market Sector (April 17) – Charles Schwab is considering entering the prediction market, but its President and CEO, Rick Wurster, believes there is a significant difference between betting on Taylor Swift's love life and betting on the latest inflation data. “At some point, we will likely launch a prediction market,” Wurster said on the company’s first-quarter earnings call on Thursday, distinguishing his bets on financial events from topics such as sports, politics, and popular culture. However, Wurster stated that Charles Schwab is not one of the companies rushing to market products related to this sector. "This isn't at the top of our client needs list, and if you look at traders' success rate data, it's not high; people usually lose money. This is definitely something we'll seriously look into, and it's quite straightforward for us to offer, and when we do, we'll make it more formal." Polkadot cross-chain bridge attacked, 1 billion DOT maliciously minted and dumped. On April 13th, according to PeckShield monitoring, 1 billion DOT tokens were abnormally minted and dumped on the Ethereum chain. On-chain data shows that about an hour earlier, attackers tampered with administrator privileges, transferring contract control to a malicious address, then immediately minted 1 billion DOT and dumped them, causing the price to plummet from $1.22 to near zero. The incident is still developing, and Polkadot has not yet issued a response. The target of this attack was a bridge asset on Ethereum, not the Polkadot native chain.
